question: Among the following who was the propo nent of the ‘Bhakti Cult’ from West Bengal? (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Chaitanya Prabhu
option_2: Ramanujacharyulu
option_3: Ramanand
option_4: Kabir
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Chaitanya Mahaprabhu (1486–1534) :

  • He was a Bengal-based saint and spiritual leader who popularized the Bhakti movement in Bengal. He was a devotee of Lord Krishna and popularized Vaishnava Bhakti by preaching the Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavata Purana.
  • Chaitanya’s mode of worshipping Krishna with bhajan-kirtan and dance had a profound effect on Vaishnavism in Bengal.
  • The Bhakti movement was a religious movement in India that began in the 7th to 10th centuries CE. The movement focused on devotion to God and sought to reform religion across all levels of society. The word “Bhakti” is Sanskrit for “devotion”.

The movement had several characteristics, including:

1. Monotheistic ideas

2. Opposition to idol worship

3. Emphasis on self-surrender

4. Emphasis on the role of Gurus

5. Promotion of universal fraternity

6. Opposition to rituals, holy places, and rest periods

  • The Bhakti cult is characterized by the attitude of the soul towards the Supreme Being.
  • The movement cut across distinctions of high and low birth, the learned and the unlettered, and opened the gateway of spiritual realization to one and all.
  • The main Bhakti cults are those of Shiva, Vishnu, and Shakti.
  • The movement became popular because it didn’t discriminate among worshippers or devotees.
  • The Bhakti movement began in South India by Alvaras and Nayanars. Alvaras are the devotees of Lord Vishnu and Nayanars are devotees of Lord Shiva. These devotees traveled to various places singing hymns in praise of their Gods.

Ramanuja (1077 – 1157) :

  • He is also known as Ramanujacharya, was an Indian Hindu philosopher, guru and a social reformer.
  • He is noted to be one of the most important exponents of the Sri Vaishnavism tradition within Hinduism.
  • His philosophical foundations for devotionalism were influential to the Bhakti movement.

Ramananda (1400–1470) :

  • He was a North Indian Brahman (priest), vedanta teacher, and poet saint. He was also known as Ramadatta or Ramanand.
  • Ramananda was a part of the bhakti movement, a Sanskrit word that means “devotion”. He is credited with reviving the Ramanandi sect and is considered a founder of the Bhakti tradition. Hindu tradition recognizes him as the founder of the Ramanandi Sampradaya, the largest monastic Hindu renunciant community today.
  • Ramananda’s teachings were similar to those of Ramanujacharya, and he followed Ramanujacharya’s Vishishtadvaita philosophy. He taught in Hindi, condemned the caste system, and allowed people from all castes to join his sect.

Kabir (1398–1518 CE) :

  • He was a prominent Indian poet, saint, and social reformer during the 15th and16th centuries. His writings influenced the Bhakti movement and are included in Sikhism’s scripture Guru Granth Sahib. Kabir’s verses are also found in the Satguru Granth Sahib of Saint Garib Das and the Kabir Sagar of Dharamdas.
  • Kabir was a proponent of the Bhakti Movement, which sought to coordinate religions by offering a universal path for all people. He is also known for his ideas about social equality and a casteless society based on rationality. Kabir’s poetry and dohas have been analyzed to understand how his works have impacted India’s social and cultural domain.
  • Kabir is revered by Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs. He was born in 1440 in Varanasi, India and died in 1518 in Maghar.
  • Kabir is said to have been raised in a Muslim weaving family known as Julahas, who lived in or around the city of Benares (Varanasi).

question: Which of the following Muslim rulers en forced price control system? (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Alauddin Khilji
option_2: Mohd. Tughlaq
option_3: lltutmish
option_4: Balban
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Alauddin Khalji (1296–1316) was the first Muslim ruler to introduce price controls in India. He set prices for a variety of goods, including animals, cloth, grains, and slaves.
  • To implement the price control system, Alauddin appointed a market inspector called Shahna-i-Mandi. The inspector’s job was to ensure that prices were not higher than the prescribed rates and to check the quality and quantity of the commodities sold in the market.
  • Alauddin also set up three markets in Delhi to control prices. He fixed the prices of the commodities and reduced their rates so that his soldiers could live comfortably.
  • Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1290 - 1351) : He was the second sultan of the Tughlaq dynasty and the 18th ruler of the Delhi Sultanate. He ruled from 1325 to 1351 and was the eldest son of Ghiyath al Din Tughluq, the founder of the dynasty. Tughlaq is known for his wild policy swings, which left his subjects impoverished.

Some of his policies included:

1. Trade and commerce: Tughlaq attempted to implement a new coinage policy, which caused a complete upheaval of trade and commerce.

2. Capital shift: He moved the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad, also known as Devagiri, in the Deccan area of India.

3. Taxes: He raised taxes in the Doab region to fill the empty treasury.

  • Tughlaq also annexed several kingdoms into his territory and defeated the Mongols who attacked the Delhi sultanate. He was known for his support of Sufi saints and followed a policy of religious tolerance and patronage.
  • Tughlaq died on March 20, 1351 while trying to chase and punish people for rebellion and their refusal to pay taxes in Sindh (now in Pakistan).

Iltutmish (1211 - 1236) :

  • He was the third and greatest Delhi sultan of the Mamluk dynasty, or Slave dynasty. He is considered the founder of the Delhi Sultanate because he was the first Muslim ruler to rule from the city.

Iltutmish’s achievements include:

1. Strengthening and expanding the Muslim empire in northern India

2. Moving the capital from Lahore to Delhi

3. Completing the Qutub Minar

4. Introducing administrative reforms such as the Iqtadari system, the establishment of an army, the introduction of coins, and the formation of Chalisa Dal

5. Carrying out expeditions on the Rajput states to extend his empire

  • Iltutmish was also known as Altamsh and Shams-al-Dīn Iltutmish. He was a Turkic slave born in Central Asia who was sold into slavery and eventually became a slave to Qutb al-Dīn Aibak. He married Aibak’s daughter and succeeded him in 1211.
  • Iltutmish died in April 1236 and was buried in a tomb he had built for himself in Delhi.

Ghiyas-ud-din Balban (1216-1287) :

  • He was a Sultan of the Mamluk (Slave) dynasty of Delhi. He ruled from 1266 to 1287 AD. Balban was known for his authoritarian rule, but also for flourishing architecture and culture. He built many Sufi shrines and invited many Sufi saints to Delhi.
  • Balban was a famous and one of the most powerful sultans of the Delhi Sultanate of India. His real name was Ulugh Khan and his famous titles were Nabāb-e-Khudā (Acolyte of God), Jhil-e-Ellāhi (Shadow of God), Khudā-e-Vandgār (Devotee of God).
  • Balban was sold as a slave to the then-ruler Iltumish in 1232 CE, who later released him. He took the throne after the death of Sultan Nasiruddin Mahmud.
  • Balban’s reign was full of terror among the nobles and public equally. He successfully recaptured Bengal and fought the invasion of the Mongol forces.
  •  Balban’s main principles of the theory of Kingship were: Kingship is the vice regency of God on earth. The king is the shadow of God.

question: “Sufi Sect” originated and developed in........... (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Christianity
option_2: Islam
option_3: Hinduism
option_4: Zoroastrianism
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Sufi sect originated and developed in Islam.
  • Sufism is a liberal reform movement within Islam that originated in Persia and spread to India in the 11th century. It is also known as Islamic mysticism or asceticism.
  • Some say that Sufism has existed as a practice of Muslims since the earliest days of Islam, even predating some sectarian divides.
  • Others trace the pre-Islamic roots of Sufism back to the early Christian mystics of Syria and Egypt, to the Essenes, the ancient Pythagorean orders, and the mystery schools of the Egyptians and Zoroastrians.
  • The Sufis are a group of Muslim mystics who started a religious reform movement in West Asia.

Their teachings include:

1. There is only one god and all people are his children.

2. Muhammad is considered to be the legitimate Sufi sheikh.

question: “Mansabdars” in Mughal period were.......... (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Landlords and Zamindars
option_2: Officials of the state
option_3: Those who had to give revenue
option_4: Revenue collectors
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Mansabdars were nobles and high-ranking military officials who held positions of power and authority in the Mughal Empire.
  • The word “Mansab” is Arabic for “position” or “rank”. The Mansabdari system was introduced by Mughal Emperor Akbar in 1571.
  • Mansabdars were appointed to all civil and military posts, and could be transferred between the two. They were an integral part of the Mughal bureaucracy, and held important offices such as wazir, bakshi, faujdar, and subadar.
  • Mansabdars were responsible for bringing a fixed number of men-at-arms, horses, and elephants to the field. They were rated based on the numbers, which were known as Zats. For example, they might be referred to as Mansabdars of 10, 20, 100, and 1000.
  • Mansabdars received their salaries as revenue assignments called Jagirs.

question: The earnings of the kings in the Medieval age were mostly derived from........... (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Offerings made at the temples
option_2: Land revenue
option_3: Trade
option_4: Industrial production
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The main source of income for medieval kings was land revenue. This was usually one-fifth of the total production, but later increased to one-half.

Other sources of revenue for medieval rulers included:

1. Taxes: Taxes on land, trade, and crafts

2. Feudal dues: Payments from nobles and vassals who owned land. In exchange for their land and protection, these people had to give money, crops, animals, or work for the king

3. Custom duties: Taxes on goods that were brought in or taken out of the kingdom

4. Pillage: Going to war and pillaging other lands

5. Fees: Fees charged to their lords

6. Shield money: Some lords paid the king “shield money” instead of going to war

  • Kings could also increase their income by managing their estates well and granting market rights to towns. They could also sell special privileges, such as fishing rights.

question: The great philosopher Shankara advocated............ (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Dvaita
option_2: Advaita
option_3: Hinduism
option_4: Altruism
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The great Indian philosopher Shankara advocated Advaita. Advaita is a Hindu philosophy that states that the soul is identical with God. It is also known as the doctrine of the oneness of the individualsoul and the Supreme God.
  • Shankara is considered one of the most influential Indian philosophers from the classical period. He is revered by Advaita Vedānta’s teaching tradition and monastic lineages, and continues to influence virtually all contemporary lineages today.
  • Shankara is also credited with unifying and establishing the main currents of thought in Hinduism. He established the importance of monastic life as sanctioned in the Upanishads and Brahma Sutra.

Dvaita :

  • It is a Sanskrit word that means “duality” or “dualism”. It refers to a concept in which two truths or principles are completely separate and independent. Dvaita is a school of Vedanta, one of the six philosophical systems of Indian philosophy.
  • Dvaita Vedanta states that god and individual souls are completely separate. For example, Dvaita posits more than one fundamental metaphysical principle to explain the universe or a fundamental distinction between the human souls and the Supreme Being.
  • Dvaita is based on the teaching of one of the great saints of India in around 13th century, Sri Madhavacharya. Madhva, also known as Anandatirtha, lived from around 1199 to 1278 and came from the area of modern Karnataka state.

Altruism :

  • It is a selfless concern for the welfare of others. It is a core aspect of many religious traditions, including Hinduism.
  • Altruism is also a key aspect of many humanitarian and philanthropic causes.

Some examples of altruism include:

1. Helping others without expecting anything in return

2. Forgoing things that may benefit oneself but may cost others

3. Sharing scarce resources

4. Helping others despite knowing the personal risks or costs involved

  • Buddhism, which originated in India, also contributed to the spread of altruistic behavior to other species.

question: The purpose of Mohammad Ghazni’s at tack on India was............ (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: To plunder the wealth of India
option_2: To spread Islam in India
option_3: To rule over the territories of India
option_4: None of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The purpose of Mohammad Ghazni’s attack on India was to plunder the wealth of India.
  • Mahmud of Ghazni, a powerful Turkic ruler, attacked India 17 times between 1000 and 1027 AD. His primary goal was to plunder the rich temples of northern India, most notably the famous Somnath Temple.
  • In 1025 AD, he raided the Somnath Temple on the coast of Kathiawad and the temple was destroyed in 1026 AD.

question: What was the important reason for the fall of Vijayanagar Empire?  (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Unity among the Muslim rulers
option_2: Internal instability and weakness of Princes
option_3: Moplah’s rebellion
option_4: Economic bankruptcy
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Vijayanagara Empire was a medieval Hindu empire that ruled much of Southern India from 1336 to 1646.
  • The empire’s power declined after a military defeat in 1565, and it is generally agreed that the empire’s fall was due to a combination of factors:
  •  Unity among Muslim rulers: The sultans of Golconda and Ahmadnagar, who had lost much to Rama Raya, may have formed an alliance to destroy Vijayanagar’s power.
  • The Battle of Talikota in 1565 is considered a major reason for the fall of the Vijayanagar Empire. The battle was fought between the Hindu king of the Vijayanagar Empire and the four Deccan Sultanates of Bidar, Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, and Golconda.
  • The battle resulted in the defeat of the Vijayanagara Empire, the capture and death of Rama Raya, and the destruction of much of the city of Vijayanagar.
  • The Vijayanagar Empire was established in 1336 and was based in the Deccan region of southern India. The empire fell in 1678.

Other reasons for the decline of the Vijayanagara Empire include:

1. Corruption

2. Complacency

3. Laziness

4. Lack of coordination and understanding in the army

  • The Vijayanagara Empire was established in 1336 by the brothers Harihara I and Bukka Raya I of the Sangama dynasty. The empire’s capital was Vijayanagara, later renamed Hampi, and its extensive ruins are now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: During Aurangzeb’s reign, which of the following were not included in his government? (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Rajputs
option_2: Pathans
option_3: Marathas
option_4: All of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • During Aurangzeb’s reign, the Marathas were not included in his government. 
  • Aurangzeb’s Deccan policy led to the destruction of the best soldiers and the Mughal Empire’s downfall. He waged a long war against the Marathas, and the Deccan wars between the Mughal Empire and the Marathas lasted from 1680 until 1707. 
  • Aurangzeb’s policies of religious persecution and attempts to extinguish the Maratha power led to the weakening and eventual downfall of the Mughal Empire. 
  • Aurangzeb initially sought to strengthen his position by forming alliances with the Rajputs. He married their princesses and appointed them to important positions in his court. 
  • However, his policies towards the Rajputs gradually became more oppressive, leading to a series of wars and rebellions.

question: “Bijanti” government is compared to the Chola’s for............ (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Rural democracy
option_2: Administrative system
option_3: Land revenue collection system
option_4: Display of wealth
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The “Bijanti” government refers to the Byzantine Empire, which was famous for its grandeur, opulence, and magnificent display of imperial wealth.
  • The Byzantine rulers projected their power and divine authority through elaborate court ceremonies, luxurious clothing, rich jewelry, golden thrones, intricately decorated palaces, and splendid churches like the Hagia Sophia. The aim was to create an aura of divine kingship and impress both their subjects and foreign envoys with the empire’s prosperity and stability.
  • Similarly, the Chola Empire in South India (9th–13th century CE) was also known for its display of immense wealth and royal magnificence.
  • The Cholas constructed grand temples such as the Brihadeeswarar Temple at Thanjavur, adorned with intricate sculptures and towering vimanas (temple towers). These temples were not only centers of worship but also symbols of royal power and economic strength.
  • The Chola kings organized grand rituals and donated immense wealth to temples in the form of gold, land, and jewels. Their art, architecture, and bronze sculptures reflected both religious devotion and a deliberate display of prosperity.

question: Whose name is associated with Fatehpur Sikri?  (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Akbar
option_2: Babar
option_3: Shah Jahan
option_4: Hamayun
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Mughal Emperor Akbar is associated with Fatehpur Sikri. Akbar founded the city in 1569 to commemorate his victory over Mewar. It served as the capital of the Mughal Empire from 1571 to 1585.
  • The city is located in the Agra District of Uttar Pradesh, India, and is named after the village of Sikri that was previously located on the site. The city is predominantly made of red sandstone and is 37 kilometers from Agra.
  • Akbar also built the Buland Darwaza at Fatehpur Sikri to commemorate his victory.

Babur (1483-1530) :

  • He was the founder of the Mughal Empire in India and the first Mughal Emperor. His real name was Zahir-ud-Din Muhammad, which means “Defender of the Faith”.
  • Babur was a descendant of the great Turkic conqueror Timur and the great Mongol conqueror Genghis Khan. He was a Chagatai Turk, a branch of the Turks named after Genghis Khan’s second son.
  • Babur was a brilliant military strategist and a skilled tactician. He was also a poet and a writer.

Babur’s military conquests include:

1504: Babur occupied Kabul

1524: Babur occupied Lahore but had to retreat to Kabul after Daulat Khan turned against him

1525: Babur attacked and occupied Punjab again

  • In the Battle of Ghagra in AD 1529, Babur defeated the Afghans on the banks of the river Ghagra after defeating the Rajputs. Babur’s power consequently encompassed nearly all of northern India.

Shah Jahan (1592 - 1666) :

  • He was the fifth Mughal emperor of India, ruling from 1628 to 1658. He was a military commander and grandson of the Great Akbar. His birth name was Shahab-ud-din Muhammad Khurram.
  • Shah Jahan is known for his contributions to Indian Mughal architecture, including the Taj Mahal, the Pearl Mosque, the Red Fort, and the Jama Masjid. These monuments combine Persian, Indian, and Islamic architectural styles. Shah Jahan was also a patron of poetry and literature flourished during his reign.
  • Shah Jahan is also known for advocating for equality between Hindus and Muslims.

Humayun (1508 - 1556) :

  • He was the second Mughal emperor of India. He ruled from 1530 to 1540 and again from 1555 until his death in 1556. Humayun was the son and successor of Babur, the founder of the Mughal dynasty.
  • Humayun is also known as Nasir-ud-Din Muhammad. During his reign, he focused on architectural development instead of expanding his empire.

Humayun’s reign can be divided into two periods:

(1) 1530-1540 AD :

  • Humayun was an inexperienced ruler who fought with his step-brothers. Sher Shah Suri attacked Mughal territories from Bengal.

(2) 1555-1556 AD :

  • Humayun besieged the fortress of Kalinjar in Bundelkhand, defeated the Afghans at Douhrua, drove out Sultan Mahmood Lodhi from Jaunpur, and defeated Bahadur Shah of Gujarat.
  • Humayun was more of an adventurer than a consolidator of his empire. He was driven from his capital of Delhi by the Afghan usurper Shēr Shah of Sūr. He had to leave India for Afghanistan and Iran.

question: Which one of the following was the cause of disintegration of the Mughal Empire? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: War of succession among sons of Aurangzeb.
option_2: Attacks of Nadir Shah and Ahmad Shah Abdali.
option_3: Revolts of various communities like Jats, Sikhs, Rajputs, etc.
option_4: All of the above mentioned factors contributed to the downfall of the Mughal Empire.
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

All of the above factors contributed to the downfall of the Mughal Empire:

Foreign invasions :

  • The invasions of Nadir Shah and Ahmad Shah Abdali drained the Mughals’ wealth and shook the empire’s stability.

Weak successors :

  • The Mughals didn’t have a succession law, so when a ruler died, his brothers would fight for the throne. This weakened the empire, especially after Aurangzeb’s death.

Aurangzeb’s policies :

  • Historians disagree on Aurangzeb’s religious views, but some say he was intolerant and wanted to destroy other religions. Others say his constant conflicts with the Marathas and Sikhs weakened the empire.

Inefficient rulers :

  • Some say the inefficiency of the later Mughal rulers was the main reason for the empire’s fall.

Other factors that contributed to the decline of the Mughal Empire include:

Aurangzeb’s long war in the Deccan:

  • This strained the empire’s military and financial resources.

Demoralization of the Mughal army:

  • This was caused by financial difficulties from continuous wars.

Decline in efficiency of the Mughal administration:

  • The later Mughal emperors couldn’t control their powerful mansabdars.

question: Who was the famous Deccan Hindu King whose fleet crossed the Bay of Bengal with an army and conquered a number of sates in Sumatra, Java and Malaysia? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Rajaraja I
option_2: Rajendra Chola
option_3: Pulakesin
option_4: Mahipala II
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Rajendra Chola I was a Tamil Chola emperor of South India who crossed the Bay of Bengal and conquered states in Sumatra, Java, and Malaysia.
  • Rajendra Chola I was the son of Rajaraja and developed a navy for expeditions to the Ganga valley, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia.
  • He successfully invaded the Srivijaya kingdom in Southeast Asia, which led to the decline of the empire there.
  • Rajendra Chola I built a new capital called Gangaikonda Cholapuram to celebrate his victories in northern India.
  • The name means “the town of the Chola who took over Ganga”. He also took the title of Gangaiknodachola, which means “conqueror of Ganga”.

question: Which dynasty was well-known for excel lent village administration? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Pandyas
option_2: Pallavas
option_3: Cholas
option_4: Chalukyas
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Uttaramerur inscriptions, written in Tamil and Granta script, provide details about the Chola administration. The inscriptions, which date from 919 921 AD, describe the functioning and constitution of the local sabha. They also provide information about the village administration, taxation, and land revenue under the Cholas.
  • The Chola dynasty was known for its excellent village administration. The Cholas were one of the longest ruling dynasties in South India’s history.
  • The Chola administrative system was known for its village autonomy, with Chola officials acting more as observers than administrators.
  • The Chola dynasty was also known for its local self-government. The king was the supreme authority of the state, and a Council of Ministers helped the king with state affairs.

The Pandyas :

  • They were an ancient Tamil dynasty that ruled over the southern part of the Indian subcontinent until the end of the 15th century. The Pandyas were one of the four great kingdoms of Tamilakam, along with the Pallavas, the Cholas, and the Cheras.
  • The Pandyas established their dynasty in southern Tamil Nadu by the end of the sixth century CE. Their main city was Madurai, where Tamil kings held three Sangam poetry academies. The Pandyas initially practiced Jainism, but later became Hindus, specifically Shaivas.
  • The Pandyas were excellent traders, with their trade routes extending from Vanish Cody and Pol Pod to Rome and Greece, and all the way up to China, Malaysia, and Maldives. They also produced the finest pearls along the South Indian coast.
  • The Pandyas were unable to oppose the Delhi Sultanate’s imperial desires

The Pallava dynasty :

  • They ruled a large portion of the Deccan, also known as Tondaimandalam, from 275 CE to 897 CE. The Pallavas were a powerful force in the South around the 4th century AD and were at the height of their power in the seventh century AD.
  • The Pallavas were patrons of art and architecture and are considered the originators of Dravidian style architecture.
  • Some of the best examples of Pallava art and architecture are the Kailasanathar Temple at Kanchipuram, the Shore Temple and the Pancha Rathas of Mahabalipuram.

The Chalukya dynasty :

  • These was a Hindu dynasty that ruled parts of central and southern India from the 6th to 12th centuries.

The Chalukyas ruled as three related but separate dynasties:

1. Badami Chalukyas :

  • The earliest Chalukya dynasty, which ruled from the mid-6th century. Their capital was Badami (Vatapi) in Karnataka. The Badami Chalukyas declined after the death of their greatest king, Pulakesin II, in 642 AD.

2. Western Chalukyas :

  • Ruled as emperors in the Deccan from 543 to 757 CE and again from about 975 to about 1189. Tailapa II (973-997 CE) is considered one of the founders of the Western Chalukya dynasty.

3. Eastern Chalukyas :

  • Ruled in Vengi (in eastern Andhra Pradesh state) from about 624 to about 1070.
  • The Chalukyas and the Pallavas were major kingdoms that ruled much of South India. The Chalukyas had their capital at the Raichur Doab located between the Krishna and Tungabhadra rivers.
  • The Chalukyas left behind more than 150 monuments, built between 450 and 700, in the Malaprabha basin in Karnataka. Their most celebrated monuments include the rock-cut temples of Pattadakal, Badami, and Aihole, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: Who were ‘Jagirdars’ during the reign of Akbar? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Large estate owners
option_2: Officials of state who were given jagir in place of cash pay
option_3: Revenue collectors
option_4: Autonomous rulers under Akbar
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • During the reign of Akbar, Jagirdars were officials of the state who were given lands, called “jagirs”, in lieu of cash pay. The Mughal emperor gave these land grants to nobles, officials, and military officers in return for their services.
  • The jagirdars collected revenues from these lands and were primarily responsible for maintaining loyalty to the emperor.
  • The jagirdar system was introduced by the Delhi Sultanate, and continued during the Mughal Empire. The land assignments to Jagirdars were hereditary.

question: What was not the most important feature of land revenue system of Akbar? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Collection of land revenue in kind or cash
option_2: Collection of land revenue based on accurate measurement of land
option_3: Collection of land revenue directly at the central treasury
option_4: Fixation of rates
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The land revenue system of Emperor Akbar, known as the Ain-i-Dahsala or Todar Mal Bandobast, was one of the most efficient and scientific revenue systems in medieval India.
  • Introduced by Raja Todar Mal, Akbar’s finance minister, this system emphasized accurate land measurement, fair assessment, and proper classification of land according to fertility and productivity. The main features of Akbar’s revenue system included:

1. Accurate measurement of land – Land was measured using standardized measuring rods called bamboo jaribs.

2. Classification of land – Land was categorized into three types (good, middling, and bad) based on fertility, and an average produce was calculated.

3. Fixation of revenue rates (dahsala system) – The average produce and prices of crops over ten years were used to fix a uniform rate of revenue, generally one-third of the produce, which could be paid in cash or kind.

4. Collection through local officers – Revenue collection was supervised by officials like amil, qanungo, and patwari at the local level, not directly by the central treasury.

  • Therefore, the collection of land revenue directly at the central treasury was not an important feature of Akbar’s land revenue system, as revenue was collected at the village and district levels and only a portion eventually reached the imperial treasury. The emphasis was on systematic assessment and fair collection, not centralization of collection.

Hence, the correct answer is (c) Collection of land revenue directly at the central treasury.

question: The Ajanta Caves were built during the period of............. (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Guptas
option_2: Kushans
option_3: Mauryas
option_4: Chalukyas
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Mauryan Empire : (4th–2nd century BCE) is far earlier than the main period of Ajanta’s development. The Mauryan period is, however, notable for Ashokan rock-cut caves, such as those in Barabar Hills (Bihar), which were simpler monastic retreats for Ajivikas and early Buddhists. These caves show polished rock interiors but lack the elaborate sculptures and paintings seen at Ajanta.
  • While the early phase of Ajanta began around the 2nd century BCE (near the end of Mauryan rule), the grand artistic and architectural features appeared much later, during the Gupta era.

question: Which statement about Amir Khusrau is not true?  (UPSC Prelims 1981)
option_1: He worked for the Hindu-Muslim Unity
option_2: He was a great poet
option_3: He was a great historian
option_4: He wrote poetry in Hindi and Urdu
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Amir Khusrau (1252-1325) was the famous Persian writer of this period and not Urdu. He also wrote some Hindi verses. So correct option is D. His famous work Tughlaq Nama deals with the rise of Ghyiasuddin Tughlaq.
  • He also catered to the cause of peaceful coexistence of Hindu-Muslim in the society.
  • He is known as the “father of Urdu literature” and is also credited with contributing to the development of the ghazal.
  • Khusrau was a Sufi poet, musician, and scholar from South Asia. He wrote poetry primarily in Persian and Hindavi, which is a combination of local Bhojpuri and Persian. Hindavi later evolved into Hindi and Urdu.
  • Khusrau is also credited with being the “founder of qawwali,” a Sufi devotional singing style. He is also credited with introducing new ragas such as ghora and Sanam, and with inventing the sitar.

question: Bijak was written by............  (UPSC Prelims 1981)
option_1: Tulsidas
option_2: Ramdas
option_3: Guru Nanak
option_4: Kabirdas
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Kabir Das wrote the famous poem Bijak. The book is a collection of the 15th-century Indian poet and saint Sant Kabir’s verses. The verses cover various aspects of life, spirituality, and human nature.
  • Bijak is a holy scripture for followers of the Kabirpanthi religion. It is one of the earliest major texts in modern Hindi.
  • Kabir was born into a Muslim family and was influenced by both Hindu and Islamic teachings. He composed his works in a mix of Hindi and Urdu.

question: Which Mughal ruler introduced the Mansabdari system in India?  (UPSC Prelims 1981)
option_1: Akbar
option_2: Humayun
option_3: Shahjahan
option_4: Babar
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Mughal Emperor Akbar introduced the Mansabdari system in 1571. The system was used in early modern India and determined the rank and status of military generals and government officials.
  • The word “mansab” is Arabic for “rank” or “position”. The system gave nobles the right to hold a revenue assignment, or jagir, for their services. The emperor had direct control over these nobles.
  • The ranks were divided into Zat, Sawar, and Chehra. Mansabdars were military commanders, high civil and military officers, and provincial governors.
  • Mansabdars whose rank was one thousand or below were called Amir, while those above 1,000 were called Amir-al Kabir.
  • Under this system, every officer was assigned a rank, or mansab. The lowest rank was 10 and the highest was 5,000 during the early reign of Akbar. Later, Akbar raised the highest rank to 10,000 and then 12,000.
  • The Mansabdari system had 33 categories of Mansabdars. The lowest Mansabdar commanded ten soldiers, while the highest commanded 10,000. Only the royal family’s princes and the most powerful Rajput rulers received a mansab of 10,000.
  • Akbar’s Mansabdari and Jagirdari system strengthened the empire’s administration. Jahangir and Shah Jahan later introduced new systems into the original Mansabdari system.

question: Why did Md-Bin-Tughlak change his cap ital from Delhi to Hyderabad/Deogiri?  (UPSC Prelims 1981)
option_1: Because he wanted to improve trade in Deccan
option_2: Because he wanted to spread Islam in the(incomplete)
option_3: Because he wanted to punish the people of Delhi
option_4: Because he wanted to escape from Mongolian invasion
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Muhammad bin Tughlaq, the second ruler of the Tughlaq dynasty, moved his capital from Delhi to Devagiri in 1327 for two main reasons:

1. Central location: To establish the capital at the center of his vast empire

2. Protection: To protect the capital from foreign invasions

  • Tughlaq renamed Devagiri as Daulatabad. However, the plan was not implemented properly, and many common people suffered. Tughlaq soon realized that it was difficult to keep an eye on northern frontiers from the new capital. He issued orders to reshift his capital to Delhi.

question:
Which of the chronological order for the construction of the following is correct?  (UPSC Prelims 1982)

1. Taj Mahal

2. Qutab Minar

3. Fatehpur Sikri

4. Agra Fort

Select the correct answer from the codes given below
option_1: 2, 4, 3, 1
option_2: 2, 4, 1, 3
option_3: 1, 2, 3, 4
option_4: 2, 3, 4, 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Qutb-ud-din Aibak, the first Sultan and Muslim ruler of Delhi, built the Qutb Minar in 1192. The tower was completed in 1368 by Firoz Shah Tughlaq.
  • The Qutb Minar is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a symbol of victory built in the early 13th century. The 72.5 meter high tower is made of red sandstone and tapers from 2.75 meters in diameter at the top to 14.32 meters at the base.
  • The Qutb Minar was damaged by lightning in 1326 and 1368, and was repaired by Muhammad-bin-Tughluq and Firuz Shah Tughluq. In 1503, Sikandar Lodi restored and enlarged the upper storeys.
  • The Mughal emperor Akbar built the Agra Fort between 1565 and 1573. The fort was built as a tribute to the Mughal empire’s splendor. It’s a crescent-shaped fortress made of sandstone with a 2.5-kilometer long enclosure wall.
  • The Mughal Emperor Akbar built Fatehpur Sikri, also known as the City of Victory, between 1572 and 1585. The city was the capital of the Mughal Empire from 1571 to 1585.
  • Akbar built the city to honor the Sufi saint Sheikh Salim Chisti. The emperor is said to have gone to Sikri to seek the saint’s blessing for a son. The city is made primarily of red sandstone and is located about 37 kilometers from Agra.
  • Fatehpur Sikri is a complex of monuments and temples, all in a uniform architectural style.
  • The complex includes the Jama Masjid, one of the largest mosques in India, and the Tomb of Salim Chishti. Other major attractions include the Buland Darwaza, the main gateway to the palace.
  • In 1986, Fatehpur Sikri was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
  • The Taj Mahal was built between 1631 and 1648 by Mughal emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife, Mumtaz Mahal. The construction of the mausoleum was mainly finished in 1643, but work on other parts of the project continued for another 10 years.
  • The Taj Mahal is an ivory-white marble mausoleum on the south bank of the Yamuna river in Agra, India. It was commissioned in 1632 by Shah Jahan, who ruled from 1628 to 1658. The Taj Mahal is considered one of the world’s heritage masterpieces and the jewel of Muslim art in India.
  • The construction project employed around 20,000 artisans under the guidance of a board of architects led by Ustad Ahmad Lahori, the emperor’s court architect.

question: Ramanuj Acharya gave stress on............. (UPSC Prelims 1982)
option_1: Bhakti
option_2: Moksha
option_3: Dharma
option_4: None of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Ramanujacharya, an Indian theologian and philosopher, stressed the importance of bhakti, or devotion to a personal God. He believed that bhakti was a means to spiritual liberation.
  • Ramanujacharya’s interpretation of Hindu scripture is known as Vishishtadvait Vedanta. He developed a different approach to Vedanta by giving the concept of bhakti an intellectual basis. His philosophical foundations for devotionalism were influential to the Bhakti movement.
  • Ramanujacharya argued that in order to attain salvation, grace of God was more important than knowledge about him. He emphasized that the path of prapatti or total reliance or surrender to God was open to all, irrespective of caste.
  • In Hinduism, there are four main paths by which a follower can reach moksha, or liberation.

These paths are :

1. bhakti, the path of devotion;

2. gnana, the path of knowledge;

3. karma, the path of selfless service; and

4. raja, the royal path of meditation.

question: The succession of Delhi Sultans to the throne was generally............ (UPSC Prelims 1982)
option_1: Hereditary
option_2: After fight between brothers
option_3: By killing the father
option_4: Invasion
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The succession of Delhi Sultans to the throne was generally hereditary. This means that the eldest son or a chosen heir would succeed the ruler upon their death. However, there was no definite law of succession. 
  • The main factor in succession to the throne was military strength between brothers. This led to political instability. 
  • The history of the Delhi Sultanate shows that the death of the monarch was usually followed by a period of uncertainty. In the absence of a law of succession, intrigues, civil war, and violence became the order of the day. 
  • The confusion reached up to this extent that sometimes a son ascended the throne while his father was still alive.

question: Which of the following statements is cor rect for Guru Nanak?  (UPSC Prelims 1982)
option_1: He preached punishment for wicked
option_2: He preached against Muslims
option_3: He preached a war society
option_4: He preached that “God is one”
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Guru Nanak preached that “God is one”.
  • Guru Nanak was the founder of Sikhism, a monotheistic religion that originated in the 15th and 16th centuries. He believed in one God who was formless and omnipresent. He also believed in the unity of God, and that all human beings are equal, regardless of caste, class, religion, belief, color, sex, and race.
  • Guru Nanak’s teachings also emphasized the equality of women and rejected the authority of the Vedas and the path of renunciation. His social teachings condemned the caste system and preached equality for all people, regardless of caste or gender.

question: In whose reign Mughal painting art was at its zenith? (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Jahangir
option_2: Babur
option_3: Shahjahan
option_4: Aurangzeb
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Mughal painting reached its zenith during the reign of Emperor Jahangir. Jahangir was a naturalist who preferred paintings of flora and fauna. He also had a deep interestin painting art, and during his reign, the Persian and Indian style of painting was fully synthesized.
  • Mughal painting is a South Asian style of painting that originated from the territory of the Mughal Empire in the Indian subcontinent. It developed in the court of the Mughal Empire of the 16th to 18th centuries.

Some characteristics of paintings during Jahangir’s reign include:

1. Liberal use of golden colors along borders with colorful floral design

2. The theme of the portraiture were mainly flora, fauna and birds

3. The paintings were highly secular in form

question: Who established four Maths’ in four cor ners of India? (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Shankaracharya
option_2: Bhaskara
option_3: Ramanujacharya
option_4: None of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Adi Shankara, an eighth-century religious scholar and philosopher, established four Hindu mathas (monasteries) in four corners of India:

1. Dwarka: Gujarat

2. Joshimath: Uttarakhand

3. Puri: Odisha

4. Sringeri: Karnataka

  • The mathas were established to uphold Sanatana Dharma (Hinduism) and propagate Vedanta. They were also meant to serve as centers for the 10 religious orders of Shankara’s group and as bulwarks for Hindu missionary activity.
  • The mathas are among the most revered pilgrim destinations in India.

question: Ramanujacharya advocated............. (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Dvaita
option_2: Advaita
option_3: Bhakti cult
option_4: Vaishnavism
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Ramanujacharya was an Indian theologian, scholar, and social reformer who advocated for the Bhakti cult and the Vishishtadvaita philosophy.
  • Ramanujacharya’s school of thought is called Monoism, which in Hindu religion translates into Vishishtadvaita Vedanta. In Vishista Advaita, both the world and Brahman are considered equally real.
  • Ramanuja advocated prabattimarga or the path of self-surrender to God. He invited downtrodden people to Vaishnavism and advocated salvation by Bhakti.
  • Ramanujacharya was also an advocate of social equality among all sections of people. He encouraged temples to open their doors to everyone irrespective of caste or position in society.

Dvaita and Advaita are two different philosophies:

1. Dvaita :

  • Means duality, and is based on the idea that the creator and nature are two separate, eternal realities. Dvaita Vedanta originated in Southern India in the 13th century by Sri Madhvacarya. Dvaita Vedanta asserts that the world is real, and that everything, including the universe and God, is real. Dvaita Vedanta also states that souls and objects cannot exist without God, but that they are separate.

2. Advaita :

  •  Means non-duality, and is based on the idea that there is only one supreme reality: God. Advaita Vedanta states that the self is identical with the ultimate reality, and that the soul is identical with God. Advaita propounds that the world is an illusion, and that all actions and emotions, including sorrow, are just false impressions.

Vaishnavism :

  • It is a major form of modern Hinduismthat involves the worship of Vishnu and his incarnations (avatars).
  • Vaishnavism is a “polymorphic monotheism” that recognizes many forms of one unitary divinity.
  • Vaishnavism is mentioned in the Vedas, which were compiled around 1500 BCE. The writing of epics like the Mahabharata and Ramayana around 500 BCE later pushed the faith forward.

Vaishnavism is characterized by:

1. Worship :

  • Vaishnavism is the worship and acceptance of Vishnu as the supreme manifestation of the divine.

2. Polymorphic monotheism :

  • Vaishnavism is a theology that recognizes many forms of one original deity.

3. Bhakti yoga :

  • Bhakti yoga requires practicing devotion to the Supreme deity that’s based on a sincere desire to please, rather than the hope of reward or the fear of punishment.

4. Eternal beings :

  • Vaishnavism acknowledges that all living beings are eternal persons, and that all life’s problems are rooted in the individual soul’s forgetfulness of his or her relationship with God.
  • A devotee of Vishnu is called a Vaishnava.

question: “Bhakti Cult” laid emphasis on........... (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Idol worship
option_2: Universal brotherhood
option_3: Devotion to God
option_4: None of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Bhakti Cult, a Hindu reform movement, emphasizes devotion to God. The movement originated in South India in the 9th century AD.

The Bhakti movement emphasizes:

1. Devotion to God

2. The unity of Hindu gods

3. Surrendering oneself to God

4. Equality and brotherhood of all people

5. The fundamental equality of all religions and individuals

6. The individual’s direct connection to God

7. The possibility of salvation for all through good deeds and simple living

The Bhakti movement also:

1. Rejected the caste system

2. Emphasized the importance of self surrender for obtaining the bliss and grace of God

3. Valued the importance of Gurus who acted as guides and preceptors

4. Preached the principle of universal brotherhood

5. Were against rituals, pilgrimages, and fasts

6. Emphasized purity of heart and mind

7. Preached monotheism

question: Which of the following is not true about the paintings of Mughal period? (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Religious scenes were depicted.
option_2: Scenes of nature were depicted.
option_3: Court and cultural scenes were depicted to balance communal harmony.
option_4: Scenes of battles were depicted.
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Mughal paintings were secular in nature and depicted themes such as:

  • Courtly life, Battle scenes, Mythology, Hunting scenes, Wildlife, Portraits.
  • Mughal paintings were a style of South Asian painting that did not include Islamic themes.
  • They were generally made as miniatures and were confined to the Mughal court.
  • Mughal artists introduced landscape painting as a distinct genre. They depicted scenes of gardens, rivers, hills, and forests, often combining them with human figures and architectural elements.

question:
Write the correct chronological order of the following events: (UPSC Prelims 1984)

A. Shifting of capital from Delhi to Daulatabad

B. First Battle of Panipat

C. Second Battle of Tarain

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: A, B, C
option_2: C, A, B
option_3: C, B, A
option_4: A, C, B
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

 The Second Battle of Tarain :

  • It was fought in 1192 between the Ghurid forces of Muhammad Ghuri and the Rajput Confederacy of Prithviraj Chauhan. The battle took place near Tarain, which is now known as Taroari in Haryana, India.
  • The battle resulted in the defeat of the Rajputs. This was the first time that a non Indian and non Hindu power emerged in Indian subcontinent. Finally this battle paved the way for Sultan rule in India.
  • The First Battle of Tarain was fought in 1191, in which the Chahamana king Prithviraj Chauhan defeated the Ghurid sultan Mu’izz al-Din.

Shifting of capital from Delhi to Daulatabad :

  • In 1328, Muhammad bin Tughluq of the Delhi Sultanate moved the capital of his kingdom from Delhi to Devagiri, which he renamed Daulatabad.
  • The name Daulatabad translates to “Abode of Wealth” or “The City of Fortune”.
  • Tughluq was impressed by the fort and wanted to strengthen his control over the Deccan. He also wanted to protect himself from the threat of Mongol invasion from North west India.
  • However, the move led to serious repercussions and Tughluq had to transfer the capital back to Delhi for various reasons.

The First Battle of Panipat :

  • It took place on April 21, 1526, near the village of Panipat in present-day Haryana, India. The battle was fought between the forces of Babur and the Lodi Empire, and marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire.
  • Babur’s forces defeated the larger army of Ibrahim Lodi, Sultan of Delhi, due to Babur’s tactics and the use of guns.
  • Babur’s new tactics included the Tulghuma and the Araba, and the sound of the canons frightened Lodhi’s war elephants. Babur also used field fortifications and the firepower of gunpowder. The Second and Third Battles of Panipat took place in 1556 and 1761.

question: Ruins of Vijayanagar empire can be seen at............ (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Hospet
option_2: Hampi
option_3: Halebid
option_4: Madurni
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The ruins of the Vijayanagar Empire can be seen at Hampi, Karnataka. In 1986, UNESCO designated the ruins at Hampi as a World Heritage Site.
  • Hampi was the capital of the Vijayanagar Empire, one of the largest empires in India’s history.
  • The empire flourished on the banks of the Tungabhadra River for more than 200 years, from the mid-14th century. The ruins of Hampi include examples of temple architecture from the Vijayanagar period.

question:
What is the correct order of the following? (UPSC Prelims 1984)

A. First Battle of Panipat

B. Second Battle of Tarain

C. Establishment of East India Company

Select the correct answer from the codes below:
option_1: B, A, C
option_2: A, B, C
option_3: C, A, B
option_4: C, B, A
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The First Battle of Panipat :

  • It took place on April 21, 1526, near the village of Panipat in present-day Haryana, India. The battle was fought between the forces of Babur and the Lodi Empire, and marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire.
  • Babur’s forces defeated the larger army of Ibrahim Lodi, Sultan of Delhi, due to Babur’s tactics and the use of guns.
  • Babur’s new tactics included the Tulghuma and the Araba, and the sound of the canons frightened Lodhi’s war elephants. Babur also used field fortifications and the firepower of gunpowder. The Second and Third Battles of Panipat took place in 1556 and 1761.

The Second Battle of Tarain :

  • It was fought in 1192 between the Ghurid forces of Muhammad Ghuri and the Rajput Confederacy of Prithviraj Chauhan. The battle took place near Tarain, which is now known as Taroari in Haryana, India.
  • The battle resulted in the defeat of the Rajputs. This was the first time that a non Indian and non Hindu power emerged in Indian subcontinent. Finally this battle paved the way for Sultan rule in India.
  • The First Battle of Tarain was fought in 1191, in which the Chahamana king Prithviraj Chauhan defeated the Ghurid sultan Mu’izz al-Din.

The British East India Company (EIC) :

  • It was established on December 31, 1600, when Queen Elizabeth I granted it a Royal Charter. The company was a joint stock company owned by British aristocrats and merchants. It was originally chartered as the “Governor and Company of Merchants of London trading into the East Indies”.
  • The EIC was established to trade with East and Southeast Asia and India. The company initially traded with maritime Southeast Asia, but later traded with China and India. The company was originally created to participate in the East Indian spice trade, but later added items such as cotton, silk, tea, and opium.
  • The EIC dominated much of the Indian subcontinent between 1611 and 1858. In 1651, the company set up the first English factory on the banks of the river Hugli in Bengal. By 1696, the company began building a fort around the Hugli settlement.
  • In 1690, the company bought the Sultanate, Gobindapur, and Kolkata to build factories. In 1717, the Mughal Empire issued a Farman, or trade license, to the company. The EIC was dissolved in 1874.

question:
The following are the rulers of the slave dynasty.......... (UPSC Prelims 1984)

1. Ghiyas-ud-din Balban

2. Iltutmish

3. Qutb-ud-din Aibak

4. Nasir ud-din Mahmud

The correct order in which these sultans ruled over India is :
option_1: 2 3 1 4
option_2: 1 4 2 3
option_3: 4 1 2 3
option_4: 3 2 4 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

Qutb-ud-din Aibak – First ruler of the Slave Dynasty (1206–1210 CE):

  • Qutb-ud-din Aibak was originally a Turkish slave of Muhammad Ghori. After Ghori’s death in 1206, Aibak declared himself the ruler of Delhi, becoming the founder of the Mamluk (Slave) Dynasty and the first Sultan of Delhi.
  • He is remembered for initiating the construction of the Qutub Minar and building Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque. His rule was short (only four years), as he died in 1210 CE after falling from a horse while playing polo.

Iltutmish – Second ruler (1211–1236 CE):

  • After Aibak’s death, Iltutmish, his son-in-law and one of his trusted slaves, ascended the throne. He was the real consolidator of the Delhi Sultanate. Iltutmish repelled both internal revolts and external threats, including Mongol invasions.
  • He also made Delhi the capital of his empire (instead of Lahore) and introduced a silver coin called Tanka and a copper coin called Jital. Importantly, he secured Caliphal recognition for his rule, strengthening his legitimacy as Sultan.

Nasir-ud-din Mahmud – Third ruler (1246–1266 CE):

  • Nasir-ud-din Mahmud was the son of Iltutmish and ruled as a nominal Sultan, while actual power was held by his capable minister, Ghiyas-ud-din Balban, who served as Naib (Deputy Sultan). Nasir-ud-din Mahmud was known for his piety and devotion to religion. His reign was largely peaceful due to Balban’s strong administrative control behind the throne.

Ghiyas-ud-din Balban – Fourth ruler (1266–1287 CE):

  • After Nasir-ud-din Mahmud’s death, Balban took the throne and ruled with an iron hand. He was a strong and autocratic ruler who believed in maintaining strict discipline and the dignity of the monarchy. His reign marked the height of centralized power in the Mamluk Dynasty. He suppressed rebellions, strengthened the spy system (barid), and introduced the idea of “Divine Kingship” — portraying the Sultan as the shadow of God (Zill-e-Ilahi).

Therefore, the correct chronological order of these rulers is:

  • Qutb-ud-din Aibak → Iltutmish → Nasir-ud-din Mahmud → Ghiyas-ud-din Balban, which corresponds to the sequence 3 – 2 – 4 – 1.

question:
Which of the following statements re garding Adi Shankaracharya is/are correct? (UPSC Prelims 1985)

I. He was one of the greatest Hindu scholars

II. He was born in Kerala

III. First ever Indian feature film in Sanskrit was made after his name

IV. The above feature film was adjudged the best film for the year 1983

V. He wrote a commentary on the Upanishads

Of these statements :
option_1: All are correct
option_2: Only I, II and III are correct
option_3: Only I, III and V are correct
option_4: All except II are correct
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

 All statements about Adi Shankaracharya are correct:

  • He was one of the greatest Hindu scholars
  • He was born in Kerala
  • First ever Indian feature film in Sanskrit was made after his name
  • The above feature film was adjudged the best film for the year 1983
  • He wrote a commentary on the Upanishads
  • Adi Shankaracharya (788–820 CE) was an Indian Vedic scholar and teacher who lived in the 8th century CE. He is considered one of India’s greatest philosophers. His writings are based on the Advaita Vedanta school of Hinduism.
  • Adi Shankaracharya is credited with unifying and establishing the main currents of thought in Hinduism.

He is also known for:

1. Establishing the importance of monastic life

2. Establishing four mathas in Sringeri, Dwarka, Puri, and Badrinath

3. Traveling across India to spread his philosophy through debates and discourses

4. Believing that the Upanishads are the only way to gain knowledge of Brahman

5. Believing that yoga could help people acquire moksha knowledge

  • The first Sanskrit film ever made was Adi Shankaracharya in 1983 by G.V. Iyer. It won four awards at the 31st National Film Awards, including Best Film, Best Screenplay, Best Cinematography, and Best Audiography.

question:
Match the following: (UPSC Prelims 1985)

A. Amir Khusro

1. 14th century

B. Kabir

2. 13th century

C. Abul Fazal

3. 15th century

D. Tukaram

4.16th century:

 

5. 17th century

 

 

 

 

 

 

Codes: ABCD :
option_1: 3 4 2 5
option_2: 5 2 1 4
option_3: 2 3 4 5
option_4: 1 3 5 4
solution_text:
Explanation :

Amir Khusrau (1253 - 1325) :

  • He was a poet, historian, and Sufi musician and scholar from the 13th and 14th centuries. He is known as one of the four great pillars of Persian literature from the 14th century.
  • Khusrau is credited with combining Persian, Arabic, Turkic, and Indian singing traditions to create qawwali, a type of Sufi devotional song. He is also credited with inventing Tarana and Trivat.
  • Khusrau is known as “Tuti-e-Hind”, or “Indian Nightingale”, in the Indian music industry. His most famous work, the khamsa known as khusro ka khamsa, was composed during the reign of Alau-din Khilji.

Kabir :

  • He was a famous 15th century Hindi poet, was born in 1425 AD in Varanasi. He was a weaver who rejected the external forms of worship of both Islam and Brahminical Hinduism. His teachings were based on a rejection of major religious traditions and he believed in Hindu-Muslim unity.
  • Kabir’s teachings influenced Guru Nanak, who founded Sikhism in the 15th century. Kabir contributed 217 songs to the Sikh holy book, the Guru Granth Sahib, more than any other Guru.
  • Kabir’s legacy continues through the Kabir Panth, a religious community that recognizes him as its founder.

Abul Fazl (1551 - 1602) :

  • He was an Indian historian, writer, and politician who lived during the 16th century. He was a friend and counselor to the Mughal emperor Akbar, and served as the empire’s grand vizier from 1579 until his death in 1602.
  • Abul Fazl is considered one of India’sgreatest historians. He wrote a detailed history of Akbar’s reign, known as Akbarnama, which includes A’in-i Akbari. A’in-i Akbari is a primary source for Akbar’s administrative institutions and explains the Mughal concept of kingship.
  • Abul Fazl was born in Agra to the Indian scholar and teacher Shaikh Mubarak. Shaikh Mubarak educated Abul Fazl in the Islamic sciences, Greek philosophy, and mysticism.

Sant Tukaram Maharaj (1608–1650) :

  • He was a 17th century Marathi poet and saint. He was a devotee of Vitthal, a form of Lord Krishna, and a member of the Bhakti movement in Maharashtra. Tukaram is known for his devotional poetry and community-oriented worship with spiritual songs called kirtans.
  • Tukaram was born in 1608 in the village of Dehu, which is now part of the city of Pune. He lived with Maratha ruler Shivaji Maharaj and saints such as Eknath and Ramdas.
  • Tukaram’s poetry is compiled in the Marathi language work Tukaram Gatha, which was likely composed between 1632 and 1650.

question: Which of the following statements is correct? (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: The village was the basic unit of Chola administration.
option_2: The Chola kingdom was on the decline in the 9th century.
option_3: Amoghavarsha is the remembered of the Chola kings.
option_4: None of the above.
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The correct statement is (a) The village was the basic unit of Chola administration:

  • The Chola village administration was well developed and systematic. The village (ur) was the basic unit of local administration, followed by the sub-region (nadu) and district (kottam). Groups of villages formed larger units called “Nadu”.
  • The village council and the nadu performedadministrative functions such as dispensing justice and collecting taxes.
  • The Chola kingdom was divided into provinces called mandalams. The mandalams were further divided into valanadus. Each valanadu consisted of a certain number of villages.
  • The Chola kingdom rose in the 9th century. Some famous Chola kings include Karikala Chola, Rajaraja Chola, Rajendra Chola, and Kulothunga Chola I.
  • The Chola dynasty ruled South India from the 9th to the 13th centuries. The dynasty’s decline began in the 12th century and continued into the 13th century.
  • The Chola empire was completely destroyed in 1310 when Malik Kafur, a general in Alauddin Khilji’s army, invaded South India.

The Chola dynasty’s decline was caused by a number of factors, including:

1. Lack of resources

2. Inefficiency

3. Corruption

4. Succession rivalries

5. Infiltration of armed forces

6. Increasing corruption and continuous expeditions

7. Local chiefs gaining power

  • The Cholas were one of the richest and strongest imperial forces of the time, but they were unable to regain their previous position.
  • Rajaraja Chola I (985-1014 CE) and his son Rajendra Chola are considered to be the best-remembered Chola kings.
  • Rajaraja Chola I is considered one of the greatest rulers in Indian history. He is known for his military conquests, architectural achievements, and patronage of the arts and sciences. During his rule, the Chola kingdom grew into an extensive and well-knit empire.
  • Rajendra Chola’s major achievement was capturing regions in north India. He took the title “gangaikonda” in memory of this conquest. He also developed a new capital called “gangaikonda cholapuram” and built a large Shiva temple.

question: Shivaji was coronated at.......... (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: Raigarh
option_2: Aurangabad
option_3: Peshawar
option_4: Bundela
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Shivaji was crowned king of the Maratha Empire at Raigad Fort on June 6, 1674. The coronation was a lavish ceremony and an important landmark in the history of the Indian subcontinent.
  • After his coronation, Shivaji assumed the title of Chhatrapati. He also married into some of the leading old Maratha families to strengthen his social position.
  • Shivaji died in April 1680, in the mountain stronghold of Raigad, which he had made his capital.

question:
Assertion (A): Hiuen Tsang came to India during Harsha’s rule.

Reason (R): His main objective was to visit the Buddhist shrines. (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: if A and R both are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: if A and R both are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
option_3: if A is incorrect but R is correct
option_4: if A is correct but R is incorrect
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Hiuen Tsang, a Chinese Buddhist pilgrim, visited India during the reign of King Harshabardhan in 630 A.D. He stayed for 15 years, studying Hinayana and Mahayana Buddhism in monasteries such as Kashmir, Sialkot, Kanauj, and Nalanda.
  • Hiuen Tsang’s main objective was to acquire awareness of Buddhism and collect its religious texts. He visited many important Buddhist sites like Lumbini, Kusinagara,Saranath, Bodh Gaya, Amaravati, Ajanta, and Nalanda.

question:
Match the columns: (UPSC Prelims 1985)

A. Amir Khusro

1. 16th century

B. Din-i lllahi

2.12th century

C. Qutub Minar

3.14th century

D. Chand Bardai

4. 13th century

 

 

 

 

 

Select the correct answer from the codes given below:

ABCD
option_1: 4 1 2 3
option_2: 4 1 3 2
option_3: 3 2 4 1
option_4: 2 3 4 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Amir Khusrau (1253 - 1325) :

  • He was a poet, historian, and Sufi musician and scholar from the 13th - 14th centuries. He is known as one of the four great pillars of Persian literature from the 14th century.
  • Khusrau is credited with combining Persian, Arabic, Turkic, and Indian singing traditions to create qawwali, a type of Sufi devotional song. He is also credited with inventing Tarana and Trivat.
  • Khusrau is known as “Tuti-e-Hind”, or “Indian Nightingale”, in the Indian music industry. His most famous work, the khamsa known as khusro ka khamsa, was composed during the reign of Alau-din Khilji.

Din-i Ilahi :

  • He is also known as Divine Faith, was a religious movement created by the Mughal emperor Akbar in 1582. The name Din-i Ilahi is Persian for “Divine Faith”.
  • The movement was an ethical system that combined Islam and Hinduism into one faith, but also added aspects of Christianity, Zoroastrianism, and Jainism. Akbar’s goal was to bring people together through a religion that served only one God.
  • The movement was an elite religious movement that never had more than 19 adherents. It did not last long or have many followers. However, it did cause conflict between the Mughal emperor and the Indian Islamic community.

The Qutb Minar :

  • The Qutb Minar, a 72.5 meter high tower made of red sandstone, was built in the early 13th century. The tower is located a few kilometers south of Delhi.
  • The Qutb Minar was built by Qutb-ud din Aibak, the first Sultan and Muslim ruler of Delhi, in 1192. Construction was completed in 1368 by Firoz Shah Tughlaq.
  • The tower is a mix of polygonal and circular shapes and is made of red and buff sandstone, with some marble in the upper stories. It’s thought that the tower is named after Qutb-ud-din Aibak, who started it. However, it’s also possible that it’s named after Khwaja Qutbuddin Bakhtiar Kaki, a 13th-century sufi saint.
  • The Qutb Minar was damaged by lightning in 1326 and 1368, and was repaired by the rulers of the day.

Chand Bardai :

  • He was a poet from medieval India who lived from 1149–1200. His epic poem Prithviraj Raso is considered one of the first works in Hindi literature. The earliest manuscript of the poem dates to the 12th century.
  • The poem is about the 12th-century Indian Rajput king Prithviraj Chauhan. It describes the love and abduction of Raja Jai Chandra’s daughter of Kannauj by Prithviraj Chauhan.
  • Some scholars date the oldest version of the poem to the 13th century, while the earliest extant copy dates back to the 16th century.

question:
Assertion (A): Akbar introduced Din-i-Il lahi which became more popular.

Reason (R): Din-i-Illahi was a religious order, suitable for the people of all religions. (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: if A and R both are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: if A and R both are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
option_3: if A is incorrect but R is correct
option_4: if A is correct but R is incorrect
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Akbar, the Mughal emperor, did not found Din-i-Ilahi. However, he did initiate a syncretic religious movement called Din-i-Ilahi in the late 16th century. The movement was not very popular and did not have a lasting impact.
  • Din-i-Ilahi was a new religion that combined Islam and Hinduism into one faith, while also incorporating aspects of Christianity, Zoroastrianism, and Jainism.

The term Din-i-ilahi translates to “Divine faith”. The movement was not successful for a few reasons:

1. Akbar’s successors were not as liberal and tolerant as he was, so they did not promote it.

2. More orthodox Muslims were not ready to accept other religious practices.

3. The movement was not suitably projected to the masses.

  • Din-i-Ilahi was open to all, and had good features of all religions. It had no religious sculptures, rituals, religious places of worship, or priestly class. Akbar used to give a Mantra in the ear of the person who wanted to join it.

question:
Assertion (A): The Cholas had a conquest of Southeast asia.

Reason (R): They had developed a very strong fleet. (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: if A and R both are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: if A and R both are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
option_3: if A is incorrect but R is correct
option_4: if A is correct but R is incorrect
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
  • The Chola dynasty (300 BC–AD 1279) was a major sea power in Southeast Asia.
  • They built a large fleet of ships and established settlements and trade missions wherever they went. The Cholas used a sophisticated maritime trade system to influence religion, culture, and architecture throughout Southeast Asia and even to China.
  • The Cholas were passionate about Indian culture, religion, and heritage. Their invasion of Southeast Asia was a sensible extension of Chola policy, and can be explained by the economic and political realities that defined South India in the medieval era.

question: Which of the following statements about Mohammad-bin- Tughlaq is correct? (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: He introduced paper currency
option_2: He introduced the postal system
option_3: He was the ablest man among the crowned heads of the medieval age
option_4: None of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The correct statement about Mohammad bin-Tughlaq is that he was the ablest man among the crowned heads of the medieval age.

Here are some other statements about Mohammad-bin-Tughlaq:

1. He introduced token currency in brass and copper, in place of gold and silver.

2. He borrowed the idea of token currency from Paper Money issued by his contemporary Kublai Khan in China.

3. He issued copper coins and kept their value at par with the gold and silver coins.

4. He introduced a religious approach ingovernance.

5. He discouraged Ulemas, soldiers, and nobles.

6. He shifted his capital from Delhi to Devagiri in central India.

7. He established the capital city at the center of the vast empire.

8. He protected the capital city from foreign invasions.

9. He created the department of agriculture (Diwani-Amirkohi).

10. He formulated famine.

11. He wanted to make Devagiri his second capital so that he might be able to control South India better.

12. He took over parts of Punjab and large parts of the Malabar coast and Telangana.

question: Which of the following statements about Hiuen-Tsang is not true?  (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: He visited India during the reign of Harshavardhana
option_2: He spent some time at Nalanda University
option_3: He advocated for strong Indo China relations
option_4: He influenced Buddhism
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The statement that Hiuen-Tsang advocated for strong Indo-China relations is not true.
  • Hiuen-Tsang was a Chinese traveler who visited India during the reign of Emperor Harsha.
  • He stayed at Nalanda University in the 7th century AD and left a detailed description of the education system and monasteries.
  • Hiuen-Tsang also provided detailed information on social, economic, and religious conditions under King Harsha’s rule.
  • He wrote about the religious condition of India, describing that Brahamanism, Buddhism, and Jainism were all popular religions.
  • He also described that there was complete tolerance among people of all religious faiths and people changed their religions voluntarily.

question: Which of the following is wrongly matched?  (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1:
Abul Fazl

‘Baburnama’
option_2:
Kalhana

‘Rajtarangini’
option_3:
Mohd Jayasi

 ‘ Padmavat’
option_4:
Abdul Hamid

‘Badshahnama’
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Abu’l-Fazl ibn Mubarak :

  • also known as Abul Fazl, wrote Akbarnama between 1590 and 1596.
  • Akbarnama is a three-part biography of the third Mughal Emperor, Akbar, who ruled from 1556–1605. Akbar himself commissioned the work, which is the official chronicle of his reign.
  • Abul Fazl was the grand vizier of Emperor Akbar from the time of his appointment until his death in 1602. In 1602, Prince Salim ordered Abul Fazl’s murder, believing him to be an obstacle to his becoming emperor.
  • After Akbar’s death in 1605, the manuscript was passed down to his son Jahangir, who ruled from 1605–1627, and then to his grandson Shah Jahan, who ruled from 1628–1658.
  • Baburnama, also known as Tuzuk-i-Babari, is an autobiography written by Zahiruddin Muhammad Babur (1483-1530).
  • Babur was the founder of the Mughal Empire in India and wrote his memoirs in his native Chagatai Turkic. The book is considered the first true work of Islamic autobiography.
  • Babur was a talented writer who also wrote many poems.
  • According to modern scholars, no other eastern prince has written such a vivid, interesting, and truthful account of his own life as Babur.
  • The book includes illustrations, some of which are contemporary views of places Babur describes. Other illustrations are taken from an illustrated copy of theBaburnama prepared for Babur’s grandson, the Mughal Emperor Akbar.

Kalhana :

  • a Kashmiri Pundit, wrote Rajatarangini (River of Kings) between 1148 and 1149.
  • The epic poem was written in Sanskrit and is a historical chronicle of early India. The name Kalhana means “Knower of Meaning” or “Reader”.
  • In his compositions, Kalhana references ancient epics, including the Mahabharata, the Ramayana, and the Vikramankadevacharita of Bilhana.

Malik Muhammad Jayasi (1477–1542) :

  • wrote the epic poem Padmavat in 1540.
  • Jayasi was an Indian Sufi poet and pir who wrote in the Awadhi language and the Persian Nastalīq script.
  • Padmavat is a famous piece of Sufi literature that tells the fictional story of the Delhi Sultan Alauddin Khalji’s desire for Padmavati, the Queen of Chittor. While Alauddin Khalji and Padmavati’s husband Ratan Sen are historical figures, Padmavati may be fictional.

Abdul Hamid Lahori :

  • wrote Badshahnama in 1648.
  • The book is a contemporary chronicle that Shah Jahan commissioned to celebrate his reign and dynasty. The book’s purpose was to establish the political and ideological legitimacy of the emperor’s rule.
  • Badshahnama is a genre of works that serve as the official history of the Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan’s reign. The book is written in three volumes, each covering a ten-year period. The first volume is based on Qazvini’s work but contains more details. The second volume covers the next ten years of Shah Jahan’s reign.
  • Lahori was a traveler and historian during Shah Jahan’s reign who later became a court historian for the emperor.

question: Who inscribed the name of Khalifa of Baghdad on his coins?  (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: Alauddin Khilji
option_2: Iltutmish
option_3: Qutub-ud-din
option_4: Balban
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Iltutmish was the first Sultan to inscribe the name of the Caliph of Baghdad on his coins.
  • Iltutmish was the third Mamluk king to rule the former Ghurid territories in northern India. He was the first Muslim ruler of Delhi and is considered the founder of the Delhi Sultanate.
  • Iltutmish was the first Turkish ruler to introduce a purely Arabic coinage. His silver coin, the tanka, weighed 175 grains and had an Arabic inscription on it. The tanka and the copper Jital were the two basic coins of the Sultanate period.
  • The Caliph may have recognized Iltutmish’s authority in India because he saw Iltutmish as an ally against his Khwarazmian rival. The Khalifa of Baghdad also bestowed the title Sultan-i-Azam on Iltutmish, which increased his position in the Muslim world.

Alauddin Khilji (1296-1316) :

  • He was a powerful Muslim ruler and Sultan of Delhi from the Khilji dynasty. He was the nephew and son-in-law of Jalaluddin Khilji, the founder of the dynasty. Khilji is considered one of the greatest rulers in Indian history.
  • He expanded the Delhi Sultanate beyond North India, conquering Gujarat and the South. He also defended his kingdom against Mongol invasions at Jaran-Manjur, Sivistan, Kili, Delhi, and Amroha.

Khilji is also known for:

  1. Keeping a description of all soldiers in his army
  2. Posting spies throughout the empire to prevent rebellions
  3. Being personally involved in the 1301 AD conquest against the King of Ranthambore, Hamir Deva
  • Khilji was poisoned with wine by MalikKafoor, who was plotting to kill him and steal the throne of Delhi.

Qutb-ud-din Aibak (1150–1210) :

  • He was the first Sultan of the Delhi Sultanate and the founder of the Mamluk Dynasty. He was born in Turkestan to Turkic parents and sold into slavery as a child. He was raised in Persia and eventually became a slave of Muhammad of Ghor, who appointed him to a military position.
  • Aibak was a general in the Ghurid army in India during the late 1100s and early 1200s. He was a fierce warrior who skillfully oversaw many battles during his life. He ruled until his death in 1210 from injuries he received during a polo match. He was laid to rest near the Anarkali bazaar in Lahore.

Ghiyas ud din Balban (1216–1287) :

  • He was a sultan of the Mamluk dynasty of Delhi who ruled from 1266–1287. He was also known as Ulugh Khan, and his titles included Nabāb-e-Khudā (Acolyte of God), Jhil-e-Ellāhi (Shadow of God), and Khudā e-Vandgār (Devotee of God).
  • Balban was a powerful sultan who is known for his strict and authoritarian rule, but also for his patronage of art and culture. He was born Bahauddin and was originally a slave purchased by Iltutmish. He quickly rose through the ranks and led successful military campaigns.
  • Balban’s reign was marked by terror among the nobles and public. He successfully recaptured Bengal and fought off the Mongol invasion. In 1285, he arranged a military defense line across the Beas River as part of his “blood and iron” fortification chain strategy.
  • Balban believed that the king was the representative of God on earth and that kingship was a divine institution. He believed that it was the king’s superhuman awe and status that ensured people’s obedience.
  • Balban was succeeded by Muiz ud din Qaiqubad, who was killed by his wazir in 1290.

question: ‘Ghuzak’ is the name of a place where...........  (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: Jayapala met his enemy Sabuktigin
option_2: Mahmud crushed defeat on Jayapala
option_3: Jayapala burnt himself on a funeral pyre since he was unable to survive the disgrace caused by his defeat at the hands of Mahmud
option_4: None of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Ghuzak is a place where Jayapala met his enemy Sabuktigin in 977 AD. The battle took place between Ghazni and Lamghan, and lasted for days.
  • During the battle, a thunderstorm broke out, and Jayapala was forced to sign a treaty that required him to pay tribute to Sabuktigin.
  • The battle took place near a mountain called the “Ukba Ghuzak”. The mountain was difficult to climb, and one of its ravines contained a clear fountain of water.

question: Which of the following is not included in Mughal paintings?  (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: Portraits
option_2: Islamic themes
option_3: Flowers and plants
option_4: Hunting
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Mughal paintings developed as a unique Indo-Persian art form during the Mughal Empire, especially under emperors like Akbar, Jahangir, and Shah Jahan. 
  • These paintings were primarily secular in nature and did not focus on purely religious or Islamic themes. Instead, Mughal artists depicted a wide range of subjects such as portraits of emperors and courtiers, scenes of royal life, hunting expeditions, battles, animals, flowers, and plants. 
  • The art was characterized by fine detailing, rich colors, and realistic representation influenced by Persian, Indian, and European styles. 
  • Islamic religious art, on the other hand, traditionally avoided human and animal figures, focusing instead on calligraphy and geometric patterns, which was contrary to the Mughal interest in portraying lifelike human figures and nature.
