  • Therefore, Islamic themes were generally not included in Mughal paintings, making option (b) Islamic themes the correct answer.

question: The supremacy and sovereignty of ‘Khal ifa’ of Baghdad was strictly observed by Muslim kings in India because............ (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: “Khalifa’ was treated as religious head of the Muslim community
option_2: ‘Khalifa’ was treated as the legal head of the Muslim community
option_3: All the Muslim States in the world used to pay tributes to him
option_4: He used to decide all the cases efficiently
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Muslim kings in India observed the supremacy and sovereignty of the Khalifa of Baghdad because all Muslim states in the world paid tribute to him.
  • The word “Khalifa” is Arabic and literally means “one who replaces someone else who left or died”.
  • A caliph is a successor to Muhammad, or generally a religious and political leader.
  • In 1229, al-Mustansir, the Abbasid caliph of Baghdad, conferred a mandate of authority on Iltutmish. This made the Sultanate of Delhi a legally and morally recognized state in orthodox Muslim eyes.
  • The Khalifa of Baghdad also bestowed the title Sultan-i-Azam on Iltutmish, which increased his position in the Muslim world.
  • This title made him the representative of the Khalifa, giving him prestige and different powers.

question: Which of the following is not true about Mughal army?  (UPSC Prelims 1987)
option_1: Mughal army was mainly standing army.
option_2: It had large cavalry.
option_3: When Mughal army used to come out, it seemed as if population of a big city is emerging.
option_4: It had large infantry.
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Mughal Empire’s army was large and permanent, consisting of about 40 lakh soldiers.
  • The Mughal emperors themselves maintained a small standing army, while the majority of the armed forces were provided by officers called mansabdars.
  • The emperor’s own household troops, called Ahadis, were recruited directly by the emperor, mainly from his own blood relatives and tribesmen.
  • The Mughal army was made up of cavalry, infantry, and artillery.
  • The artillery was subordinate to the infantry and cavalry, and the Mughals also used elephants.
  • The Mughals introduced light and medium cannons, which were easy to maneuver and could be used in open battlefields.

question: Musical instrument used by Amir Khusro was............. (UPSC Prelims 1987)
option_1: Sitar
option_2: Sarod
option_3: Tabla
option_4: Flute
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Amir Khusrau is credited with inventing the sitar, a plucked stringed instrument used in Hindustani classical music. The sitar has a long neck and a gourd-shaped body.
  • Khusrau is said to have modified the three stringed Tritantri Veena into a Setar, which eventually became the sitar.
  • Most historians believe the sitar was developed from the Setar, an Iranian instrument. Others believe Khusrau developed it from the Veena.
  • The sitar became popular in the wider world in the late 1950s and early 1960s through the work of Ravi Shankar. In the mid- to late 1960s, the sitar became popular in Western popular music, appearing ontracks by bands like the Beatles and the Rolling Stones.
  • Amir Khusrau is also credited with introducing other instruments, such as the khyal, tarana, Naqsh, Gul, Qaul, and Qalbana, into Hindustani classical music.

question: Who among the following Sultans declared himself as a lieutenant of Caliph? (UPSC Prelims 1987)
option_1: Balban
option_2: Iltutmish
option_3: Ferozeshah Tughlaq
option_4: Muhammad bin Tughlaq
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq, the Sultan of Delhi from 1325–1351, declared himself a lieutenant of the Caliph.
  • The Tughlaq dynasty ruled India from 1320–1413 and saw major changes in domestic and foreign policy.
  • Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq was the eldest son of Ghiyas-ud-Din-Tughlaq, the founder of the dynasty.
  • In the Islamic world, the Caliph was the leader of the community and the guardian of religion and political order.

question: Alauddin Khilji maintained control on price during his reign because......... (UPSC Prelims 1987)
option_1: He wanted to keep large army at lower expenses
option_2: He had mercy with poor people
option_3: He wanted to gain popularity
option_4: All of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Alauddin Khilji maintained price control during his reign to keep a large army at lower expenses.
  • He introduced a market control policy to make goods cheaper so he could pay his soldiers low salaries. This allowed him to maintain a large army on limited resources.

Alauddin’s market policy included:

1. Market division :

  • He divided the market into three categories, including a central grain market and subsidiary control shops.

2. Price control :

  • He fixed prices for all types of grain, pulses, cloth, slaves, cattle, and horses. He also fixed prices for daily necessities like meat, fish, dry fruit, sugar-cane, vegetables, needles, colors, betel-leave, silk, horse, and shoes.

3. Market administration :

  • He appointed separate officials called Sahaan-e-mandi to ensure regulations were followed in markets.
  • Alauddin also made taxation from the lower to the higher classes mandatory without any bias.

question: The court language of Mughals was......... (UPSC Prelims 1987)
option_1: Urdu
option_2: Arabic
option_3: Persian
option_4: Turkish
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The court language of the Mughals was Persian. The Mughal rulers were influenced by Persian culture and language due to their diplomatic ties and roots in Persia.
  • The Akbar period was known as the Renaissance of Persian literature, and many Sanskrit texts were translated into Persian at the court of Akbar.
  • Urdu replaced Persian as the court language in the late 19th century. The Adalat System, established in 1772 by Warren Hastings, was a combination of British and Mughal courts.
  • Urdu became the language of the lower munsif’s courts in this system.

question: Who among the following accepted Din-i- Ilahi?  (UPSC Prelims 1987)
option_1: Raja Man Singh
option_2: Birbal
option_3: Raja Tansen
option_4: Raja Todarmal
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Birbal was the first Hindu and the only Hindu courtier of Akbar to accept Din-i Ilahi.
  • Din-i-Ilahi was a religion founded by the Mughal Emperor Akbar. The main goal of Din-i-Ilahi was to promote morality, ethics, and kindness among Muslims.

Other followers of Din-i-Ilahi during Akbar’s time include:

1. Shaikh Mubarak

2. Shaikh Faizi

3. Jafar Beig

4. Qasim Khan

5. Abu’l-Fazl ibn Mubarak

6. Azam Khan

7. Abdus Samad

8. Mulla Shah Muhammad Shahadad

Raja Man Singh I (1550 - 1614) :

  • He was a Rajput ruler and the 24th Maharaja of Amber from 1589 to 1614. He was also a member of Akbar’s court and one of the “nine jewels” (Nauratan).
  • Man Singh was a trusted general of Akbar and fought in many battles, including the Battle of Haldighati against Maharana Pratap in 1576. He also served as the Subahdar of Bengal for three terms from 1595 to 1606 and the Subahdar of Kabul from 1585 to 1586.
  • Man Singh was appointed governor of Bihar and later of Bengal. He chose the site for his capital in 1595–96 because of its strategic command of the Teliagarh Pass and the Ganges River.
  • Raja Vikramjit of Gwalior gave Tansen the title Tansen. Tansen was an Indian musician, poet, and vocalist who was born around 1500 in Behata or Gwalior, India. He was a court musician in the darbar of Raja Ramachandra of Bandavagarh (Rewa).
  • Tansen is an important figure in the North Indian tradition of Hindustani classical music. He is known for his dhrupad and raga compositions, and for his vocal performances. Akbar, the emperor,considered Tansen one of the Navaratnas Nine Ministers (the nine jewels) and gave him the title Mian, an honorific, meaning learned man.
  • Tansen was said to have married Akbar’s daughter Mehrunissa. He has five children, Surat Sen, Sarat Sen, Tarang Khan, Bilwas Khan, and Saraswati, who all became accomplished musicians.
  • Tansen died at the age of 83, around 1589, and was buried at Gwalior next to the tomb of Mohammad Ghaus. Many musicians make pilgrimages to his tomb to seek his blessings.

Raja Todar Mal (1500 - 1589) :

  • He was the Finance Minister (Diwan-i Ashraf) of the Mughal Empire during the reign of Akbar I. He was also the Vakil-us Sultanat (Counsellor of the Empire) and Joint Wazir.
  • Todar Mal is known for introducing a new revenue system called zabt and a taxation system called dahshala. He surveyed crop yields and prices from 1570–1580, and based on this information, he fixed taxes on each crop in cash.
  • The dahsala system was used in most of Akbar’s empire, including Lahore, Allahabad, Malwa, and Gujarat. It was considered one of the most effective and efficient revenue and record systems.
  • Todar Mal was also one of the nine jewels of Akbar. He was a Khatri Rajput who served in his youth under the administration of Sher Shah Suri.

question: Who among the following Bhakti lead ers used the medium of dance and songs (Kirtans) to make one feel the personal presence of god near him? (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Shankaradeva
option_2: Chandidasa
option_3: Jnanadeva
option_4: Chaitanya Mahaprabh
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Chaitanya Mahaprabhu was a 15th centuryBhakti leader who used dance and songs (kirtans) to make people feel the presence of God. He was a popular Vaishanv saint and reformer from Bengal who believed that God could be realized through love, devotion, song, and dance.
  • Mahaprabhu’s style of worshipping Krishna through dance and song had a significant impact on Bengali Vaishnavism. He popularized Vaishnava Bhakti by preaching the Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavata Purana. The Bhakti movement spread throughout India in the second millennium.

Some other famous saints from the Bhakti movement include:

  • Adi Shankracharya, Meera Bai, Sant Gyaneshwar, Sant Tukaram, Bhakta Kanakadasa, Purandara dasa, Bhakta Khumbhar, Basvanna, Akka Mahadevi.

Srimanta Sankaradeva (1449–1568) :

  • He was a 15th–16th century Assamese polymath. He was a saint-scholar, poet, playwright, dancer, actor, musician, artist, and social-religious reformer.
  • Sankaradeva is considered one of the leading proponents of Vaishnavism in Assam in the late 15th century. His teachings, known as the Bhagavati dharma, are based on the Bhagavad Gita and the Bhagavata Purana. They focus on absolute surrender to the supreme deity, in this case, Lord Vishnu.
  • Sankaradeva’s Bhakti movement aimed to provide spiritual education to all people of society. He influenced two medieval kingdoms, the Koch and the Ahom kingdoms. The assembly of devotees he founded evolved into monastic centers known as Sattras. These remain important socio-religious institutions in Assam and, to a lesser extent, North Bengal.
  • Sankaradeva also began the practice of setting up namghars or houses of recitation and prayer.

Chandidas (1339–1399) :

  • He was a medieval Bengali poet from India, or possibly more than one. He wrote over 1250 poems related to the love of Radha and Krishna in medieval Bengali

Jnanadeva (1275 - 1296) :

  • He was a 13th-century Marathi poet, philosopher, and mystic. He was a key figure in the Bhakti movement, a spiritual revival in medieval India that focused on devotion to the god Krishna.
  • Jnanadeva is also known as Jnaneshwar, Dnyandev, Mauli, and Sant Dnyaneshwar. He is considered a major figure in Marathi literature, and his philosophy is based on the concept of “Jnana” or “Self-Realization”. His poetic works and philosophy are still studied and revered today.
  • Jnanadeva is known for composing the Bhavarthadipika, a Marathi oral verse translation and commentary on the Bhagavadgita. The Bhavarthadipika is also known as the Jnaneshvari. Jnanadeva also wrote a commentary on the Bhagavad Gita called Gnaneswari.

question: Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Abul Fazal-Ain-i-Akbari
option_2: Badauni - Akbar Noma
option_3: Abdul Hamid Lahori - Tabkat i-Akbari
option_4: Nizamuddin Ahmad-Badshah Nama
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Abul Fazl was a court historian for Emperor Akbar and wrote Ain-i-Akbari and Akbarnama in the 16th century. Ain i-Akbari is the third and final volume of Akbarnama, which is the official history of Akbar’s reign.
  • Ain-i-Akbari is a detailed account of the Mughal Empire’s administration.
  • Abdul Hamid Lahori was a student of Abul Fazl and wrote two volumes of Padshahnama, the official history of Shah Jahan’s reign.

question: The Mausoleum of Sher Shah is at......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Rohtas
option_2: Agra
option_3: Sasaram
option_4: Lahore
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Mausoleum of Sher Shah Suri is located in the Sasaram town of Bihar, India. The tomb was commissioned by Sher Shah Suri, the founder of the Suri Empire, and completed in 1545. It is considered a fine example of Afghan architecture in India.
  • The tomb is a majestic example of ancient architecture, featuring elements of Indo Islamic and Afghan architecture. It includes large open courtyards, high domes, and pillars. The tomb is also located in the middle of a water body, with its reflection visible from the corners.
  • The tomb is open from sunrise to sunset. Some say the best time to visit is morning or evening, as it can be hot at noon. In winter, noon is recommended.

question: Akbar found the Din-i-Ilahi primarily to.......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Put an end to the differences between the Hindus and the Muslims
option_2: Establish a national religion which would be acceptable to both the Muslims and the Hindus
option_3: Ensure racial and communal harmony.
option_4: Found a religious club.
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Akbar set up Ibadat khana at Sikri Fatehpur to discuss religion with various religious scholars.
  • He was not satisfied with any of the religions and the setup and new religion called Din-e-Ilahi.
  • Akbar used to call it Tauhid-e-Ilahi.
  • There were no religious sculptures, no rituals, no religious place of worship, and no priestly class.
  • It was open to all and had good features of all the religions.
  • He used to give a Mantra in the ear of the person who wanted to join it.
  • Most Nobels refused to join this religion but Birbal joined.

Therefore, the correct answer is B.

question:
Match the columns : (UPSC Prelims 1988)

 List-1

List-II

A. Qutub Minar

1.  Mohamed Adil Shah

B. Gol Gumbaj

2. Iltutmish

C. Buland Darwaza

3. Aurangzeb

D. Moti Masjid

4. Jahangir

Select the correct answer from the codes given below:

ABCD
option_1: 5 1 3 4
option_2: 2 4 5 1
option_3: 4 3 2 1
option_4: 2 1 5 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The construction of the Qutub Minar was started by Qitub-ud-Din Aibak, but he only constructed the basement. The construction of the tower was later taken over by his successor Iltutmish who constructed three more stories. The last two storeys were completed by Firoz Shah Tuglak.
  • Gol Gumbaz is the mausoleum of king Mohammed Adil Shah, Sultan of Bijapur. The tomb is located in Vijayapura, Karnataka. It was started in 1626 and completed in 1656. The name is based on Gola gummata derived from Gol Gombadh meaning “circular dome”.
  • Buland Darwaza or the loft gateway was built by the great Mughal emperor, Akbarin 1601 CE at Fatehpur Sikri. Akbar built the Buland Darwaza to commemorate his victory over Gujarat.
  • The Moti Masjid is a white marble mosque inside the Red Fort complex in Delhi, India. The name translates into English as “Pearl Mosque.” Located to the west of the Hammam and close to the Diwan-e Khas, it was built by the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb from 1659-1660.

question: The ‘Char Minar’ is in......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Hyderabad
option_2: Ahmednagar
option_3: Ahmedabad
option_4: Fatehpur Sikri
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Charminar, is located in Hyderabad, India. The monument was built in 1591 by Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah, the fifth Sultan of the Qutb Shahi dynasty. The name translates to “Four Towers”.
  • The Charminar is a 16th-century mosque with four grand arches, 48-meter-tall minarets, and views of the Laad Bazaar. It is located on the banks of the Musi River and is a global icon of Hyderabad. The Charminar’s long history includes a mosque on its top floor for more than 425 years.

Fatehpur Sikri :

  • It is a small city in India’s Agra district, located 37 kilometers west of Agra. It was built by the Mughal emperor Akbar in the 16th century to honor the Sufi saint Sheikh Salim Chisti. Fatehpur Sikri is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and is known as the “city of victory”.
  • Fatehpur Sikri is the first planned city by the Mughals and was the capital of the Mughal Empire from 1572 to 1585. The city is made up of a complex of temples and monuments, including the Jama Masjid mosque, one of India’s largest mosques.
  • The city’s architecture is a testimony to the Mughal Empire’s architectural achievements, and features sloping tile roofs that are a stone translation of village hut roofs.

question:
Match the columns: (UPSC Prelims 1988)

A. Namdeva

1. West Bengal

B. Chaitanaya

2. Uttar Pradesh

C. Surdas

3. Maharashtra

D. Nanak

4. Punjab

select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 3 1 2 4
option_2: 2 3 1 4
option_3: 4 2 1 3
option_4: 1 4 3 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Namdev (1270 - 1350) :

  • He was a Marathi Vaishnav saint from Narsi, Hingoli, Maharashtra. He was a leading poet-saint of the Indian medieval period, who wrote in the Marathi language. Namdev is also known as Nam Deo, Saint Namdev, or Sant Namdev.
  • Namdev was born to a religious artisan (tailor/calico-printer) Hindu family in the Marathi-speaking region (modern Maharashtra) of the then Deccan state. He was influenced by Vaishnavism and rose to prominence in India for his devotional hymns set to music (bhajan-kirtans).
  • Namdev’s life is shrouded in mystery. However, some say that Namdev, with other ascetics, visited all parts of the country and arrived in Punjab.
  • He reached Village Ghuman, where Gurdwara Darbar Sahib is situated and resided for over 17 Years.

Chaitanya Mahaprabhu :

  • He was born in 1486 in Nabadwip, Nadia, West Bengal. Nabadwip is now in present day West Bengal.
  • Chaitanya was a Hindu mystic and a saint in the Krishna Bhakti cult. He is considered an incarnation of Lord Krishna and a proponent of the Vedantic philosophy of Achintya Bheda Abheda.
  • Chaitanya’s mode of worshiping Krishna through dance and song had a significant impact on Vaishnavism in Bengal. He is also known for popularizing devotion to Lord Krishna.

Surdas :

  • He is a 15th century North Indian poet and devotee of the Bhakti Movement. He is known for his lyrics about Krishna, which are considered to be some of the best examples of Brajbhasa, a literary dialect of Hindi.

Guru Nanak :

  • the first Sikh Guru, was born on April 15, 1469 in the village of Rāi Bhoi Kī Talvandī, which is now Nankana Sahib, Pakistan.
  • Nanak was born into a Hindu merchant caste and worked as a storekeeper until a spiritual experience led him to leave his job and family. He is said to have been curious about philosophical questions, such as the meaning of life and religion, from an early age.
  • Nanak is buried by Muslims and cremated by Sikhs and Hindus.
  • A Gurdwara is located at his death place on the banks of the Ravi river in Pakistan, which is a sacred site for all, especially Sikhs.

question: Mahmud Gawan was the Prime Minister in........... (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Gujarat kingdom
option_2: Kingdom of Kashmir
option_3: Bahmani kingdom
option_4: Kingdom of Mysore
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Mahmud Gawan (1411–1481) was the prime minister of the Bahmani Sultanate of Deccan.
  • The Bahmani Kingdom was a Persianized Muslim state in South India that existed from 1347–1527 AD. It was the first independent Islamic kingdom in South India.
  • The Bahmani Kingdom was founded by Ala-ud-Din Hasan Bahman Shah, also known as Zafar Khan. The kingdom’s capital was first Gulbarga, and later Bidar.
  • The Bahmani Kingdom was divided into many provinces called Tarafs. Each Taraf was controlled by a governor called a Tarafdar or Amir. The Sultans followed a Feudal type of administration. The Bahmani Sultans adopted the administrative structure of the Delhi Sultanate.
  • The Bahmani Kingdom was often at war with the neighboring Hindu Kingdom of Vijayanagara. The kingdom’s power reached its peak under Mahmud Gawan, who served as a prime minister and general to several sultans. Gawan extended the empire by reconquering Goa, which was under the Vijayanagar Empire.

The Bahmani Kingdom declined due to:

  •  Constant war: The Bahmani and Vijaynagara rulers were frequently at war.
  •  Weak successors: After the death of Muhammad Shah-III, the successors were weak and inefficient.
  •  Foreign nobles: Foreign nobles and regional rival rulers were heavy on the Bahmani Kings.

The Bahmani Sultanate gradually broke up into five independent kingdoms:

1. Bijapur,

2. Ahmadnagar,

3. Berar,

4. Golkonda,

5. Bidar.

  • Gawan was a Persian merchant and military officer in the Bahamani military. He was appointed as Vakil-i-Sultanate in 1463 and served as prime minister for 20 years. Gawan was an able administrator who divided provinces into eight administrative units. He also founded the Madrasa of Mahmud Gawan, an Islamic college in Bidar, Karnataka, India in the 1460s.
  • Gawan was executed by Muhammad Shah III, but the Sultan regretted it until his death in 1482.

The Gujarat Sultanate :

  • It was a medieval Indian kingdom established in the 15thcentury. It was primarily located in the present-day state of Gujarat, India.
  • Zafar Khan (Muzaffar Shah) established the Gujarat Sultanate after declaring independence from the Sultanate at the end of the 14th century. Zafar Khan was appointed governor of Gujarat in 1391. He defeated Farhat-ul-Mulk near Anhilwada Patan and made the city his capital.

Some of the major dynasties that ruled Gujarat include:

  •  Chavda dynasty: Ruled in the 8th century and founded Champaner
  •  Gurjara Pratiharas: Ruled from the 8th to the 9th century
  •  Muslim rule: Ruled for four centuries after Allauddin Khilji’s invasion in 1297 AD

The Kingdom of Mysore :

  • It was a southern Indian kingdom that was founded in 1399. It was located at the meeting point of the Eastern and Western Ghats. The kingdom was ruled by the Wadiyar dynasty from 1399 to 1950, with a brief hiatus in the late 1700s.
  • The kingdom was a feudatory of the Vijayanagara Empire until 1565. In 1565, Mysore became an independent state under the Hindu Wodeyar dynasty. In 1761, Hyder Ali overthrew the dynasty and established his own control over the state. In 1782, Tipu Sultan succeeded Hyder Ali.

The kingdom’s history includes:

  •  Literature: The kingdom’s era is considered an important age of Kannada literature.
  •  Music: The kingdom was a center of Carnatic music.
  •  Architecture: The kingdom’s architecture includes palaces, courtly buildings, and temples.

The Kingdom of Kashmir :

  • It was a princely state that was under the British East India Company’s rule from 1846 to 1858.
  • Kashmir was ruled by Hindu dynasties until 1346, when it came under Muslim rule. The Muslim period lasted nearly five centuries, ending when Kashmir was annexed to the Sikh kingdom of the Punjab in 1819 and then to the Dogra kingdom of Jammu in 1846.
  • The first Muslim ruler of Kashmir was Shah Mir, who inaugurated the Shah Mir dynasty in 1339. The Mughal Empire ruled Kashmir from 1586 until 1751, and the Afghan Durrani Empire ruled from 1747 until 1819. During the Hindu period, Hinduism and Buddhism developed in Kashmir.

question: Most popular Sufis were the.......... (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Rabia
option_2: Hallaj
option_3: Ghazali
option_4: All of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Sufism is a mystical form of Islam that emphasizes spiritual closeness with God and introspection. It’s also known as Islamic mysticism or asceticism.
  • Sufis reject outward religiosity and emphasize love and devotion to God. They also emphasize compassion towards all fellow human beings.

Sufism’s practices include:

1. Prescribed daily prayers

2. Ritual chanting of God’s attributes (zhikr)

3. Veneration of saints

4. An emphasis on personal and emotional religious experiences

5. Renunciation of worldly things

6. Purification of the soul

7. Mystical contemplation of God’s nature

8. Seeking spiritual learning known as tariqa

  • Sufis believe that God’s essence is seen in the existent human being, as God is the object and human beings the mirrors. This means that since humans are mere reflections of God there can be no distinction or separation between the two.
  • The term “Sufi” is probably derived from the Arabic word “suf” which means “one who wears wool”. This is because woollen clothes were generally associated with ascetics.
  • Sufi orders are brotherhoods or mystical orders that have been present in Muslim societies for more than 12 centuries. They are deeply embedded in the cultures of many Muslim communities in Europe.

All of the following are popular Sufis:

1. Rabia alAdawiyya alQaysiyya: A Muslim saint and Sufi mystic who is also known as Hazrat Bibi Rabia Basri

2. Mansur alHallaj: A Persian mystic poet and teacher of Sufism who is known for saying “I am the Truth”

3. Al-Ghazali: A well-known and influential Persian Muslim philosopher of the Sunni Islam

4. Mansur alHallaj : is considered by some to be the most famous Sufi. He was executed in Baghdad in 922, and his death is considered a watershed moment in Sufism.

question: Which of the following is located in Aurangabad? (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Shahjahan’s tomb
option_2: Tomb of Aurangzeb’s queen
option_3: Akbar’s tomb
option_4: Babur’s tomb
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Bibi Ka Maqbara, or “Tomb of the Lady”, is located in Aurangabad, Maharashtra, India. It was built in 1660 by the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb to honor his first wife, Dilras Banu Begum. The tomb is said to resemble the Taj Mahal.
  • The Tomb of Aurangzeb is located in Khuldabad, Aurangabad district, Maharashtra.
  • Shah Jahan’s tomb is located in the Taj Mahal in Agra, India. The Taj Mahal is a white marble mausoleum built between 1631 and 1648 by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife, Mumtaz Mahal.
  • The tomb is located on a plinth
  • with a minaret at each corner of the square garden, known as the Persian “char bagh”. The graves of Shah Jahan and Mumtaz Mahal are located underground, beneath two “false” tombstones, and are asymmetrically placed in the center of the monument.
  • Akbar’s tomb is the mausoleum of the Mughal emperor Akbar. It’s located in Sikandra, a suburb of Agra, Uttar Pradesh, India.
  • Akbar himself is believed to have chosen the site of the tomb and planned the structure during his lifetime. Akbar’s son, Jahangir, finished the tomb in 1613, using red sandstone and marble. The tomb is pyramidal in shape and has five storeys.
  • Babur’s tomb is located in the Gardens of Babur, Kabul, Afghanistan. The tomb is a square structure with four narrow arched openings, carved latticework, and a taller central arch. The tomb also has a rectangular plan, raised platform, arch shape, and minarets attached at the corners.
  • Babur was the first Mughal emperor and died in Agra in 1530 from an unknown illness. He was initially buried in Agra, but his widow transferred his body to Bagh-e Babur around 1544.

question: Mansabdari system was introduced by............ (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Akbar
option_2: Jahangir
option_3: Sher Shah
option_4: Balhan
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Mansabdari system was introduced by Mughal Emperor Akbar. The system was implemented as a modern administrative machinery and tax system. It was also a combined status that showed a noble’s civil and military capacity.
  • The Mansabdari system was taken from the system followed in Mongolia. The word “mansab” is of Arabic origin and means “rank” or “position”. The system determinedthe rank and status of a government official and military generals. Every civil and military officer was given a “mansab”, which determined their salaries and allowances.
  • The Mansabdari system allowed Akbar to maintain control over a large army and ensure a sense of loyalty among his officers. It also helped in the efficient collection of revenue and the smooth functioning of the empire.

question:
Consider the following European mis sions during the Mughal period............ (UPSC Prelims 1990)

1. Thomas Roe

2. Tavernier

3. Rudolf Acquaviva

The correct chronological order of their visits is :
option_1: 1-2-3
option_2: 2-3-1
option_3: 2-1-3
option_4: 3-1-2
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The correct chronological order of the European missions during the Mughal period is 3-1-2.

 Sir Thomas Roe :

  • He was an English diplomat and member of the House of Commons who served as the first English ambassador to the Mughal court from 1615–1619. His mission was to ensure the safety of the British East India Company’s factory in Surat.
  • Roe’s arrival in 1615 was a significant event in Indian history and the first step towards establishing the British East India Company as a power in the subcontinent. Roe was successful in securing a royal charter that gave the Company the freedom to trade throughout the Mughal territory.
  • Roe’s mission faced difficulties from the beginning due to the poor quality of gifts he brought for the emperor. He spent almost four years at the Mughal court, but returned to England in 1619 without the trade agreement he sought.

Jean-Baptiste Tavernier (1605-1689) :

  • He was a French gem merchant and traveler who visited India six times between 1630 and 1668. He is best known for discovering the 116-carat diamond.
  • Tavernier’s second journey began in September 1638 and lasted until 1643. During this expedition, he traveled to the court of Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan and made his first trip to the diamond mines.
  • Tavernier’s travels and experiences are described in his two-volume book Les six voyages de Jean Baptiste Tavernier (The six voyages of Jean Baptiste Tavernier), which was published in 1676. An abridged English translation of the book was published in 1677.
  • Tavernier described the social life in India during the Mughal period as hierarchical, with status determined by caste and high social status for priests and kings. He also said that sati and child marriage were common practices.

Rodolfo Acquaviva (2 October 1550 – 25 July 1583) :

  • He was an Italian Jesuit missionary and priest in India who served the court of Akbar the Great from 1580 to 1583. He was killed in 1583 and beatified in 1893.

question:
Consider the following pairs: Vaisnava Saints: Region of strong following : (UPSC Prelims 1990)

1. Ramanujacharya : South India

2. Chaitanya : Central India

3. Vallabhacharya : Gujarat and Brajarea

4. Ramanand : North India

Of these pairs:
option_1: 1, 2 and 3 are correctly matched
option_2: 2, 3 and 4 are correctly matched
option_3: 1 and 4 are correctly matched
option_4: 1, 3 and 4 are correctly matched
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Ramanujacharya, also known as Ramanuja, was born in 1017 CE in Sriperumbudur, a small village in Tamil Nadu. He was a Vedic philosopher, social reformer, and theologian who traveled across India advocating for equality and social justice. His ultimate goal was to incorporate the Vedic way of life into society.
  • Ramanujacharya is considered the founder of the Vishishtadvaita philosophy.

His philosophical theses include:

1. Consciousness is always consciousness of some object distinguished by a characteristic

2. Consciousness is itself a property

3. Individuals are real

  • Ramanujacharya’s commentaries on the Vedas, Brahma-sutras, and Bhagavadgita provided an intellectual basis for the practice of bhakti (devotional worship).

Chaitanya Mahaprabhu :

  • was born in 1486 in Nabadwip, Nadia, West Bengal. Nabadwip is now in present-day West Bengal. Chaitanya was a Hindu mystic and a saint in the Krishna Bhakti cult. He is considered an incarnation of Lord Krishna and a proponent of the Vedantic philosophy of Achintya Bheda Abheda.
  • Chaitanya’s mode of worshiping Krishna through dance and song had a significant impact on Vaishnavism in Bengal. He is also known for popularizing devotion to Lord Krishna.

Vallabhacharya :

  • was born in 1479 to a brahmin family in Nathdwara, Rajasthan, India. He is the founder of the Shuddhadvaita Vedanta philosophy and the Vallabhacharya devotional sect. Vallabhacharya is also known as the writer of sixteen “stotras” (tracts) and several commentaries on the Bhagawata Purana.

Ramananda :

  • was a 14th-century Vaishnava devotional poet saint who lived in the Gangetic basin of northern India. He was also a North Indian Brahman (priest) who lived from around 1400 to 1470.
  • Ramananda is also known as Swami Ramanand, Ramadatta, and Ramanand. He is credited for bringing the Ramanandi sect back to life. The Hindu tradition recognizes him as the founder of the Ramanandi Sampradaya, the largest monastic Hindu renunciant community in modern times.
  • Ramananda was a vedanta teacher and a part of the bhakti movement. The word “bhakti” is Sanskrit for “devotion”.
  • Ramananda established a Vaishnava religious school based on love and devotion. He traveled to several places in North India and popularized the worship of Rama and Sita.

question: Who among the following, was known as a “Nirguna” reformer? (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: Chaitanya
option_2: Kabir
option_3: Surdas
option_4: Tulsidas
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Kabir and Nanak were the main proponents of Nirguna, a group of poet-saints who praised God without form or attributes.

Nirguna bhakti :

  • It is the worship of a formless God. The word “nirguna” comes from the Sanskrit words “nir”, which means “without”, and “guna”, which means “properties”. The term also includes the Sanskrit word “bhakti”, which means “devotion” or “faithfulness”.
  • Nirguna bhakti is a part of the Bhakti movement, which has two divisions:

1. Nirguna and

2. Saguna.

  • Saguna worship is the worship of God with form, while Nirguna worship is the worship of God without form.
  • The ultimate goal of Nirguna Bhakti is to achieve enlightenment or moksha, which is the liberation from the cycle of birth and death.
  • Nirguna poet-saints rejected the Brahmins’ supremacy, all caste-based customs, and the practice of idolatry. They valued personalencounters with God, and although they referred to him by various names and titles, their god was formless, everlasting, non incarnate, and ineffable.
  • Nirguna Bhakti is devotion that lacks any desire for personal gain and is not motivated by any of the three gunas:

1. Sattva: Purity, harmony, goodness

2. Rajas: Passion, driven, egoistic

3. Tamas: Negative, disorder, lethargy

  • Kabir (1440-1518) was a powerful figure in the 15th century monotheistic movements. He was a member of a weaver family who converted to Islam.
  • Nirguna saints also opposed the caste system and religious discrimination. They encouraged religious tolerance and preached Hindu-Muslim unity.

question: During the Mughal period, under the Zabti system, land revenue was assessed and was required to be paid........... (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: In cash or kind
option_2: Only in cash
option_3: Only in kind
option_4: By the zamindar on behalf of the peasants
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • During the Mughal period, the Zabti system was the standard system for assessing land and fixing revenue. Under this system, peasants were given the option to pay land revenue in cash or kind, whichever was more convenient. However, cash was the preferred method of payment.

The Zabti system :

  • It was a land revenue system developed during the Mughal period in India. The system was also known as the Dahsala system and was introduced by Raja Todarmal, a minister for Akbar. The term “zabti” means “fixed” or “systematic”.

The Zabti system included:

1. Cultivated yields

2. Pricing of individual crops

3. Measurement of the land

4. Fixing the cash revenue of the crop yielding process

5. Fixed rupees for payment in crop cultivation

6. Fixed land rate of the cultivating system

  • The system was developed by Akbar as a solution to the inefficient revenue system of the previous rulers. Under the system, revenue circles were created within provinces and revenue rates were fixed for specific crops.

The Zabti system had three methods for working:

1. Batai: Sharing the crop after the harvest

2. Khatt-batai: Assessing the crop while it was still standing in the field

3. Lang-batai: Assessing the crop after it was separated from the shafts

question: Kalhana’s Rajatarangini (twelfth century) contains.......... (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: A collection of stories preaching morals
option_2: The history of the dynasties of Kashmir
option_3: The history of early Muslim rule in India
option_4: An account of the early Indian voyages
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Kalhana’s Rajatarangini is a historical chronicle written in Sanskrit verse by a Kashmiri Brahman in the 12th century.
  • The word Rajatarangini translates to “River of Kings”. The book is an epic poem (mahakavya) that contains nearly 8,000 verses spread across eight books or sections.
  • The book covers the history of the Kashmir region from its earliest times to the date of its composition. It is different from other historical works because it describes not only the deeds of people but also tries tounderstand and explain the conditions of people at that time.

question: Ramanuja, the Vaishnava Saint, was born at.......... (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Srirangam
option_2: Srivilliputtur
option_3: Udipi
option_4: None of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Ramanujacharya, the Vaishnava Saint, was born in 1017 in Sriperumbudur, a village in present-day Tamil Nadu, India. His mother was Kantimathi and his father was Kesava Samayaji.
  • Ramanujacharya is a Vedic philosopher and Indian theologian and teacher. His biography is based on mythology passed down through the generations. His followers in the Vaishnava tradition wrote hagiographies, some of which were composed centuries after his death.

question:
Which of the following were the causes for the rapid dissolution of the Mughal empire? (UPSC Prelims 1991)

1. Degeneration of the Mughal nobility

2. The absence of the Law of Succession

3. Foreign invasion

Select the correct answer from the codes given below.
option_1: 2 and 3 only
option_2: 1 and 3 only
option_3: 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Mughal Empire declined over a century, beginning in the 18th century.

The empire’s decline was caused by a number of factors, including:

  •  Internal strife: The empire faced internal strife, economic downturn, and external pressures.
  •  Economic decline: The empire faced economic bankruptcy and the emperors failed to develop weaponry and battle methods.
  •  Military weakness: The Mughal army was demoralized and weakened.
  •  Foreign invasion: The empire faced foreign invasion attempts.
  •  Weak successors: The empire had many weak successors who fell prey to the plots and intrigues of the nobility.
  •  Degeneration of the nobility: The empire faced the degeneration of the nobility and corruption of the nobility.
  •  Aurangzeb’s policies: The empire faced the nature and policies of Aurangzeb, including his religious persecution of Hindus.
  •  The size of the empire: The empire’s immense size resulted in thinning resources and weakening the center.
  •  Regional powers: The empire faced challenges from regional powers. The Mughal Empire’s decline was ultimately sealed by British colonial expansion.

question: Which one of the following was the name of salt tax in ancient India under the ad ministration of Cholas? (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Bhaga
option_2: Uppayam
option_3: Valiayam
option_4: Hiranya
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The name of the salt tax in ancient India under the administration of Cholas was Uppayam.
  • The Chola Empire had many taxes, and articles were taxed based on their value. For example, oil was taxed as Ennai Vari, and pearls were taxed as Muthuvaanam. Another tax imposed by the Chola kings was Vetti, which was a form of forced labor tax.
  • Vetti was not collected in cash, but instead in the form of forced labor. Under this tax, peasants were forced to work for the kingor their village landlord for free.

question: One of the major towns in the Satavahana kingdom was............ (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Arikkamedu
option_2: Pratishthana
option_3: Korkkai
option_4: Maski
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Pratishthana, also known as Paithan, was a major town in the Satavahana kingdom. It was the capital of the first Satavahana king, Simuka, and is one of the few inland towns mentioned in the first-century Greek book, Periplus of the Erythraean Sea.
  • Pratishthana and Amaravati were the capitals of the Satavahana dynasty. The Satavahanas patronized Prakrit more than Sanskrit, and agriculture was the backbone of their economy.
  • Korkai was the Satavahanas’ main port, located near the confluence of Thampraparani with the Bay of Bengal. It was famous for pearl fishery and chank diving.

question: The official associated with land revenue collection in the Maratha administration in medieval India was............ (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Patel
option_2: Kulkarni
option_3: Mirasdar
option_4: Silhadar
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The official associated with land revenue collection in the Maratha administration in medieval India was the Patel. The Patel was the village headman and the most important Vatandar of the village.
  • The Patel was responsible for the welfare of the villagers and the village. The Patel was also sometimes required to collect revenue from the farmers.
  • The Patel was assisted by a Kulkarni who was a village accountant. The Kulkarni collected taxes and handled all the revenue or income that was produced at the village level. The Kulkarni was a Brahmin and received many perquisites like the Patel.

question: Among the following Mughal rulers who was the founder of Mansabdari System?  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Akbar
option_2: Jahangir
option_3: Shahjahan
option_4: Aurangzeb
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Mughal emperor Akbar introduced the Mansabdari system in 1595-96 CE. The word “mansab” is Arabic for “rank” or “position”. The system determined the rank and status of government officials and military generals.
  • The Mansabdari system was a military unit within the Mughal Empire’s administrative system.
  • It granted nobles the right to hold a jagir, or revenue assignment, for services rendered. The emperor had direct control over these nobles.
  • The Mansabdari system determined the salaries and allowances of civil and military officers. The term “manasabadar” refers to a person who has a mansab.
  • The Mansabdari system was borrowed from Mongolia. Abul Fazl helped Akbar introduce the system.

question: Akbar’s mausoleum is situated at........... (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Sasaram
option_2: Sikandra
option_3: Agra
option_4: New Delhi
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Akbar’s mausoleum, also known as Akbar’s Tomb, is located in Sikandra, a suburb of Agra, Uttar Pradesh, India. It’s situated on 119 acres of land, 10 kilometers from Agra. The tomb is pyramidal in shape and has five stories. The cenotaph is below ground level, and the false cenotaph is on the top floor. The entire tomb is made of red sandstone, except for the top story, which is made of white marble.
  • The tomb was built between 1605 and 1613 by Emperor Akbar’s son, Prince Jahangir. The tomb is surrounded by a walled enclosure and has a gate known as Buland Darwaza, which translates to “gateway of magnificence”. The gate has a large archway and four marble minarets.

question: The Russian traveler who visited the Dec can during the medieval period of Indian history was........... (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Rastyannikov
option_2: Shirokov
option_3: Pavlov
option_4: Nikitin
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Afanasy Nikitin was a Russian merchant who visited the Deccan during the medieval period of Indian history. He traveled to India from 1466–1472, when the Muslim Bahmani Sultan ruled the Deccan.
  • Nikitin was one of the first Europeans to travel to India and document his visit. He left his hometown of Tver in 1466 and traveled by land and sea to Mumbai, where he spent three years touring India. His diary, The Journey Beyond the Three Seas, describes his commercial journey.
  • Nikitin visited the Bahmani kingdom during the reign of Muhammad Shah III. He observed that the common people were miserable, while the nobles lived in luxury.

question:
Assertion (A): Babur wrote his memoirs in Turki.

Reason (R): Turki was the official language of the Mughal Court. (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The correct answer is C. A is true but R is false.
  • Babur wrote his memoirs, Tuzuk-i-Baburi, in the Chagatai language, which he called “Turki”. The memoirs were originally written in Babur’s mother tongue, and were later translated into Persian four times. The Persian version is called Baburnama.
  • However, Persian was the official language of the Mughal court. This is because many Persian artists, craftsmen, and scholars settled in India during the Mughal period.

question: Which one of the following is considered to be the world’s greatest iconographical creations made by the sthapatis of south India, particularly during the Chola period? (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Mahishasuramardini
option_2: Nataraja
option_3: Rama
option_4: Somaskanda
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Nataraja bronze icon is considered to be the world’s greatest iconographical creation made by the sthapatis of south India, particularly during the Chola period.
  • The Nataraja sculpture is famous for its beauty and spiritual significance. It symbolizes Shiva as the lord of the cosmic dance of creation and destruction.
  • The Chola period saw the perfection of the Dravidian style of architecture, with the construction of enormous temples in a monumental style.
  • The walls of Chola temples, such as the Tanjore and Gangaikondacholapuram temples, contain many large, finely executed icons.

question: Which one of the following is a monu ment constructed by Sher Shah?  (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Kila Kuhna Mosque at Delhi
option_2: Atala Masjid at Jaunpur
option_3: Bara Sona Masjid at Gaur
option_4: Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque at Delhi
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Qila-i-Kuhna Mosque, also known as the Mosque of the Old Fort, is a monument built by Sher Shah Suri in 1541. The mosque is located inside the Purana Qila (Old Fort) in Delhi, India.
  • Sher Shah Suri built the mosque for his own use after defeating Humayun and capturing the fort and Agra city. The mosque is considered a symbol of his royal aspirations.
  • The mosque is a large, single-aisled mosque that would have served as the sultan’s Jami, or Friday mosque.
  • The prayer hall inside measures 51.20m by 14.90m and has five arched prayer niches or mihrabs set in its western wall.
  • The mosque is considered an architectural marvel of the Pre-Mughal era and features a beautiful fusion of building materials such as red sandstone, granite, and marble.

The Atala Masjid :

  • It is a 14th-century mosque in Jaunpur, Uttar Pradesh, India. Sultan Ibrahim Shah Sharki built it in 1408 AD, on the foundations laid by Sultan Firuz Shah Tughluq III in 1356.

Bara Sona Masjid :

  • It is also known as Baraduary Masjid, is a rectangular brick and stone structure in the Malda district of India. It’s the largest remaining structure in Gaur, a ruined city on the border of India and Bangladesh.
  • The name Bara Sona Masjid translates to “Great Golden Mosque” because its domes were originally gilded with gold.
  • Sultan Nusrat Shah built it in 1526 AD. The mosque is 50.4 meters by 22.8 meters and has 11 arched openings. It’s located on the western side of a raised quadrangle, with an entrance gateway and a lake facing it. The east and north gateways remain, forming an open entrance courtyard.

The Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque :

  • It is also known as the Great Mosque of Delhi or the Qutub Mosque, is located in Delhi’s Qutub complex.
  • The mosque’s name translates to “Glory of Islam”. The mosque’s construction began in 1197 by Qutb-ud-din Aibak, the founder of the Slave dynasty, and is made from the remains of 27 demolished Jain temples. The mosque’s architecture includes cloisters around a courtyard, an arched facade, and a sanctuary to the west. The mosque is built from red sandstone, gray quartz, and white marble, and is thought to be inspired by the iron “Pillar of the Law” that stands in the courtyard.

question: Consider the following passage: In the course of a career on the road span ning almost thirty years, he crossed the breadth of the Eastern hemisphere, vis ited territories equivalent to about 44 modern countries, and put behind him a total distance of approximately 73, 000 miles. The world greatest traveler of pre modern times to whom the above passage refers is............ (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Megasthenes
option_2: Fa Hien
option_3: Marco Polo
option_4: Ibn Battuta
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The passage refers to Marco Polo, the world’s greatest traveler of pre-modern times.
  • The Eastern Hemisphere is the half of the planet that is east of the prime meridian and west of the antimeridian. It can also refer to Afro-Eurasia and Australia, and is sometimes called the “Oriental Hemisphere” or “Old World”.

question: Who among the following introduced the famous Persian Festival of NAUROJ in India?  (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Balban
option_2: Iltutmish
option_3: Firuz Tughlaq
option_4: Ala-ud-din Khilji
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Balban was the first ruler to introduce the Persian festival of Nauroj in India. Naurojis the Persian and Iranian New Year, and is celebrated on the first day of spring. The festival is also known as Nowruz, and is celebrated in many countries, including Iran, Afghanistan, and India.
  • Balban introduced the festival to impress people with his wealth and power. The festival is a Zoroastrian tradition that dates back about 3,000 years. It was celebrated by Zoroastrians in Persia, who migrated to India in the 7th century.

question: Who among the following is said to have witnessed the reigns of eight Delhi Sul tans?  (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Ziauddin Barani
option_2: Shams-i-Siraj Afif
option_3: Minhaj us Siraj
option_4: Amir Khusrau
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Amir Khusrau is said to have witnessed the reigns of eight Delhi Sultans, from Balban to Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq.
  • Amir Khusrau (1253-1325) was a Persian poet, musician, and scholar who lived during the medieval period of Indian history. He is known as the “Father of Qawwali” and is considered an iconic figure in the cultural history of the Indian subcontinent. He is also known for his historical accounts, literary work, and innovation in Indian music.
  • Amir Khusrau is said to have served under seven different Turkish Sultans in Delhi. He was a courtier, royal poet, and court musician under the Turkish Sultans.

question:
Consider the map given below: (UPSC Prelims 1994)

The shaded area in the map shows the kingdom of the :
option_1: Satavahanas
option_2: Chalukyas of Vatapi
option_3: Rashtrakutas
option_4: Marathas
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Maratha Empire or the Maratha Confederacy was a power that dominated a large portion of the Indian subcontinent in the 18th century. The empire formally existed from 1674 with the coronation of Chhatrapati Shivaji and ended in 1818 with the defeat of Peshwa Bajirao II.
  • The Maratha Empire brought an end to the chaos that prevailed in the Deccan Plateau, as a result of the expansion and advent of the Mughal Empire into south India.

The Satavahanas :

  • It was an ancient Indian dynasty that ruled from the mid-1st century BCE to the early 3rd century CE. The dynasty’s capital was Pratishthana (modern Paithan) in the Aurangabad district of Maharashtra. The Satavahanas ruled over present-day Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Telangana. They also ruled over parts of Gujarat, Karnataka, and Madhya Pradesh.
  • The Satavahanas were the first native Indian kings to issue their own coins. The coins had the rulers’ portraits on them and the coin legends were in Prakrit. Some reverse coin legends are in Tamil, Telugu, and Kannada. The most common feature of the Satavahanas’ coinage is the arched hill or Hill Symbol, popularly known as Chaitya symbol.
  • The Satavahanas’ official language was Prakrit, but the script was Brahmi. The Satavahanas also used Sanskrit in political inscriptions, but rarely.

Some important rulers of the Satavahana Dynasty include:

  •  Simuka: The founder of the Satavahana Dynasty. He built Buddhist and Jain temples.
  • Satakarni I: The third king of the Satavahanas. He was the first to expand the empire through military conquests.
  •  Vijaya Satakarni: The last ruler of the Satvahana dynasty.
  • The Satavahanas restored peace throughout the Deccan area after the Maurya dynasty and fought off foreign invasions.

The Chalukyas of Badami, also known as the Chalukyas of Vatapi :

  • It was a dynasty that ruled parts of Southern and Central India from 543 to 753 CE. Their capital was Vatapi, which is now known as Badami and is located in the Bijapur district of Karnataka.
  • The Chalukyas of Badami were the first of three related Chalukya dynasties. The other two dynasties were the Eastern Chalukyas and the Western Chalukyas. The Eastern Chalukyas ruled from the death of Pulakesin II in 642 AD until the 11th century. The Western Chalukyas were descendants of the Badami Chalukyas.
  • The Chalukyas of Badami ruled over an area that included the entire state of Karnataka and most of Andhra Pradesh. Their rule was a landmark in the history of Bagalkote.
  • The Chalukyas of Badami period was a time of Hindu culture and spirituality revival. The kings encouraged Vaishnavism, Shaivism, Shakta cults, and Jainism. Women were also respected in society. The Chalukyas came to an end in 753 AD when Kirtivarman II was deposed by Dantidurga.

The Rashtrakutas :

  • They were a royal dynasty that ruled parts of the Indian subcontinent from the 6th to the 10th century. The name “Rashtrakuta” is Sanskrit for “country” and “chieftain”. The Rashtrakutas were small clan heads in different parts of India during the time of the Mauryan Emperor Ashoka the Great (3rd century BCE).
  • The Rashtrakutas were a powerful polity that controlled territories in north and south India for almost 200 years. The Rashtrakutas promoted and expanded north Indian traditions and policies in south India. The Rashtrakutas were a medieval Hindu dynasty that ruled much of the Deccan and southern India.
  • The Rashtrakutas were officers of Rashtra (province) under the early Chalukyas of Badami. The Rashtrakutas had a monarch and feudatories based administration. The realm became more feudalized with each Rashtrakuta king’s reign.
  • The Rashtrakutas’ contributions include the Kailasanatha temple at Ellora, the Kashivishvanatha temple, and the Jain Narayana temple at Pattadakal. The Kailasanatha temple is carved out of a massive block of rock and has figures of elephants and lions on the central face of the plinth.
  • The Kashivishvanatha temple and the Jain Narayana temple are both UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

question: Who among the following Indies wrote a historical account during the Mughal period? (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Gulbadan Begum
option_2: Nurjahan Begum
option_3: Jahanara Begum
option_4: Zebunnissa Begum
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Gulbadan Begum is the only woman historian to have written a historical account during the Mughal period. She was the youngest daughter of Emperor Babur and wrote Humayun Nama, a memoir about her brother Humayun’s life and reign. The book provides insights into the Mughal Empire’s political and social history during the 16th century.
  • Begum’s account provides details on both Mughal courtly and private life, unlike other historians of the time who focused on political history. In 1899, Annette Beveridge, a Persian scholar, translated Humayun Nama into English and the Royal Asiatic Society published it in London in 1902. The original manuscript is preservedin the Museum of the Asiatic Society in Kolkata.

question: The first Indian ruler to organize Haj pil grimage at the expense of the state was........... (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Alauddin Khilji
option_2: Feroz Tughlak
option_3: Akbar
option_4: Aurangzeb
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Akbar was the first Indian ruler to organize Haj pilgrimage at the expense of the state.
  • Akbar was the third Mughal emperor and a tolerant ruler. He removed the pilgrim tax on bathing at holy sites and abolished the jaziya tax. He also provided pilgrims with subsidies.

question: The Mansabdari System introduced by Akbar was borrowed from the system fol lowed in............. (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Afghanistan
option_2: Turkey
option_3: Mongolia
option_4: Persia
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Mansabdari system introduced by Akbar was borrowed from the system followed in Mongolia.
  • The Mansabdari system is a rank-based system that assigns each individual a specific rank, known as a mansab. The mansab determines their status, salary, and military responsibilities. The word “mansab” is of Arabic origin and means rank or position.
  • The Mansabdari system originated in the reign of Changez Khan. The system was a significant part of the Mughal army. Akbar needed a strong standing army for his territorial expansion to maintain his hold over it.
  • In the Mughal Empire, a Mansabdar was an official who held a rank in the Mansabdari system. Mansabdars were responsible for both military and civil administration. They were granted jagirs (land grants) in lieu of their salaries.

question:
Who among the following were famous jurists of medieval India............. (UPSC Prelims 1995)

1. Vijnaneshvara

2 Hemadri

3. Rajshekhara

4. Jimutarvahana

Choose the correct answer from the codes given below :
option_1: 1, 2 and 3
option_2: 2, 3 and 4
option_3: 1, 2, and 4
option_4: 1 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  •  The correct answer is Option 3: I, II, and IV.

I. Vijnanesvara:

  • Vijnanesvara, also known as Vijnaneshwara, was a famous jurist of medieval India. He was a Brahmin scholar from the Chalukya dynasty and lived during the 12th century. Vijnanesvara is renowned for his work called the “Mitakshara,” which is a commentary on the Yajnavalkya Smriti, an important legal text in Hindu law. The Mitakshara is considered one of the most significant texts in the field of Hindu law and has greatly influenced legal practices in medieval India.

II. Hemadri:

  • Hemadri, also known as Hemadpant, was another prominent jurist of medieval India. He lived during the 13th century and was a minister in the court of the Yadava dynasty. Hemadri is well-known for his work called the “Chaturvarga Chintamani.” This text covers various aspects of law, including civil and criminal law, as well as topics related to governance, ethics, and social customs. Hemadris Chaturvarga Chintamani is considered an important legal treatise in medieval India.

III. Jimutavahana:

  • Jimutavahana was a renowned jurist and scholar from medieval India. He is associated with the legal text called the “Dayabhaga,” which is a commentary on the Yajnavalkya Smriti. The Dayabhaga focuses on inheritance law and property rights within the framework of Hindu law. Jimutavahanas work, along with Vijnanesvara`s Mitakshara, has had a significant impact on the development and understanding of legal practices in medieval India.

Rajasekhara :

  • Rajasekhara who was not a famous jurist of medieval India. Rajasekhara, also known as Kavi Rajasekhara, was a Sanskrit poet and playwright, not a jurist.

question:
Consider the map given below : (UPSC Prelims 1995)

The route indicated in the map was followed, during the course of the military exploits by :
option_1: Chandragupta
option_2: Harshavardhana
option_3: Rajendra Chola
option_4: Malik Kafur
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Malik Kafur was originally a Hindu eunuch of Gujarat. Malik Kafur was responsible for the conquest of the South. Ala-ud-Din put Malik Kafur in charge of the Deccan campaigns.

question:
Examine the map given below : (UPSC Prelims 1995)

The places marked 1,2,3 and 4 were respectively the seats of powers of the :
option_1: Scindias; Holkars; Gaekwads and Bhosale
option_2: Holkars; Scindias; Gaekwads and Bhosale
option_3: Gaekwads; Bhosale; Scindias and Holkars
option_4: Scindia; Holkars; Bhosale and Gaekwads
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Scindia (or Shinde) and Holkar dynasties were both prominent Hindu Maratha clans from Maharashtra who rose to power during the 18th century as part of the Maratha Confederacy. The Scindia clan established its rule over Gwalior, forming one of the strongest Maratha states in northern India during the 18th and 19th centuries. 
  • Similarly, the Holkar dynasty, also a Maratha royal house, ruled as Maratha Rajas and later as the Maharajas of Indore in central India. They remained influential until 1818 when the Maratha Empire was annexed by the British.
  • The Gaekwad (or Gaikwad) family, belonging to the Kunbi Maratha clan, ruled the princely state of Baroda in western India from the early 18th century until Indian independence in 1947. They were known for their administrative reforms and relative autonomy under British suzerainty.
  • The Bhosale family, another prominent Maratha clan, established their own branch at Nagpur, which became an important center of power in central India.
  • The main line of the Bhosales produced Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj, the founder of the Maratha Empire, and his successors continued to rule as Chhatrapatis (emperors or maharajas) from their capital at Satara after the Mughal-Maratha struggles.

question: The ‘Modi scripts was employed in the document of the............ (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Wodeyars
option_2: Zamorins
option_3: Hoyasalas
option_4: Marathas
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Modi script, also known as the Modi lipi, was used in the documents of the Marathas. The Maratha Empire existed from the 17th to the 19th centuries.
  • The Modi script is a historical script used to write the Marathi language. It was primarily used for maintaining revenue and administrative records.
  • The Modi script is a cursive script that was in use from the 15th century. It is sometimes used to write Konkani, Hindi, Gujarati, Kannada, Telugu, and Sanskrit.

 The Modi script was used to write documents related to:

  • Land revenue, Judiciary, Justice, Donation of land, Religious matters, Property matters, Jahagir vatan, Military orders.

The Wodeyars, also known as the Wadiyars :

  • They were a South Indian Hindu royal family that ruled the Kingdom of Mysore from 1399 to 1947. The Wodeyars were originally based in Mysore city and were from the Urs clan. The word “Wadiyar” means “Lord” or “Lordship” in Kannada.
  • The Wodeyars profited from the conflict between the Mughal Empire and the Marathas in the 17th century. In 1610, the Wodeyars seized Seringapatam (now Shrirangapattana) and later acquired Bangalore (now Bengaluru). The city of Mysore became the kingdom’s capital in 1799.
  • After the death of Tipu Sultan in 1799, the Wodeyars ruled as allies of the British crown from 1799 to 1947.

The Zamorins :

  • They were a Hindu ruling dynasty that ruled over the Malabar coast for over a century. The Zamorins were the hereditary rulers of Calicut, a major trading center in southern India. The Zamorins lived in a large family residence called a kovilakom, which was similar to a palace. The Zamorins followed a matrilineal system, where the eldest member of the kovilakom became the Zamorin when the previous Zamorin passed away.
  • The Zamorins originated in Nediyirippu in Eranad, which is now part of the Malappuram district in Kerala. The Zamorins dealt with Chinese, Arabs, Portuguese, and others during their reign. The final Zamorin of Calicut committed suicide by setting fire to his palace and burning himself alive inside it.

The Hoysalas :

  • They were a Kannadiga dynasty that ruled most of what is now Karnataka between the 10th and 14th centuries. The dynasty’s capital was Dwarasamudra (Halebeedu), and the Hoysalas ruled over much of South India for nearly 200 years.
  • The Hoysalas were feudatories of the Chalukyas of Kalyana. The dynasty was founded by Sala, who was succeeded by Vinayaditya and Balalla 1. The greatest ruler of the Hoysala dynasty was Vishnuvardhana (Bittideva), who defeated the Cholas in the battle of Talakadu.
  • The Hoysalas built many spectacular temples, both Hindu and Jain. The Vijayanagar dynasty succeeded the Hoysalas.

question: The sufi saint who maintained that devo tional music was one way of coming close to God was............ (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Muin-ud-din Chishti
option_2: Baba Farid
option_3: Saiyid Muhammad Gesudaraz
option_4: Shah Alam Bukhari
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The sufi saint who maintained that devotional music was one way of coming close to God was Option 1: Muin-ud-din Chishti.
  • Muin-ud-din Chishti, also known as Khwaja Gharib Nawaz, was a prominentsufi saint of the Chishti order in India. He lived during the 12th and 13th centuries and is widely regarded as one of the most influential sufi saints in the subcontinent.
  • One of the key aspects of Muin-ud-din Chishtis teachings was his emphasis on devotional music, specifically Qawwali. Qawwali is a form of mystical music and poetry that originated in the sufi tradition and is characterized by its vibrant melodies and spiritual lyrics. Chishti believed that through Qawwali, individuals could experience a profound connection with God and attain spiritual enlightenment.
  • Chishtis approach to spirituality was inclusive and accessible to people from all walks of life. He believed that music had the power to uplift the soul, purify the heart, and create a deep sense of love and devotion for God. Qawwali gatherings, known as “sama,” were organized in his dargah (sufi shrine) in Ajmer, Rajasthan, where people would gather to listen to the melodious verses and experience spiritual ecstasy.
  • Chishti`s teachings on the importance of devotional music were influenced by his belief in the power of sound and its ability to transcend worldly boundaries. He saw music as a means to dissolve the ego and achieve a state of spiritual union with the divine. Through the mesmerizing melodies and poetic expressions of Qawwali, Chishti encouraged his followers to embark on a journey of self-discovery and surrender themselves to the divine presence.
  • It is worth mentioning that while other sufi saints, such as Baba Farid, Saiyid Muhammad Gesudaraz, and Shah Alam Bukhari, also made significant contributions to sufi traditions and spirituality, they may have emphasized different aspects or practices within the sufi path. However, in the context of the question, Muin-ud din Chishti is specifically associated with promoting devotional music as a means of approaching God.
  • In summary, Muin-ud-din Chishti, also known as Khwaja Gharib Nawaz, maintained that devotional music, particularly Qawwali, was one way of coming close to God. His teachings on the transformative power of music continue to influence sufi traditions and inspire individuals to seek spiritual enlightenment through the medium of sound and devotion.

question: Nastaliq was.............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: A Persian script used in medieval India
option_2: A raga composed by Tansen
option_3: A cess levied by the Mughal rulers.
option_4: A manual of code of conduct for the Ulemas
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Nastaliq was a Persian script used in medieval India. It is a calligraphic script that originated in Persia (modern-day Iran) in the 14th century. Nastaliq is characterized by its elegant and flowing style, which is especially suitable for writing Persian and Urdu languages.
  • Nastaliq is a Perso-Arabic script used as the preferred style for a variety of languages, including Dari and Pashto in Afghanistan, and Persian in Iran. It is one of the main calligraphic hands used in writing the Persian alphabet, and traditionally the predominant style in Persian calligraphy.
  • Nastaliq is used mainly for Persian poetical writings and in Urdu and Malay manuscript. It remains one of the most expressive forms of aesthetic refinement in Persian culture to this day.

question: In medieval India, Mansabdari system was Introduced mainly for............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: making recruitment to the army
option_2: facilitating revenue collection
option_3: ensuring religious harmony
option_4: effecting clean administration
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Mansabdari system was introduced in medieval India by the Mughal Emperor Akbar in the late 16th century. The system was primarily used for militaryadministration and territorial command to sustain the army. It was also a hierarchical ranking system that encompassed both military and civil.
  • The Mansabdari system’s primary purpose was to facilitate the recruitment of soldiers into the Mughal army. Under this system, every officer or noble was assigned a rank or Mansab, which determined their military and administrative responsibilities.

The main features of the Mansabdari system were:

  • Zat: This rank indicated the officer’s position in the administrative hierarchy.
  •  Sawar: This rank determined personal pay.
  •  Mansabdars: These were classified into three classes based on the ratio of their zat and sawar ranks.

question: Mughal painting reached its zenith under............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Humayun
option_2: Akbar
option_3: Jahangir
option_4: Shahajan
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Mughal painting reached its zenith under the reign of Jahangir, the fourth Mughal Emperor. Jahangir was a naturalist who preferred paintings of animals, birds, flowers, and trees. He also continued the tradition of portraiture.
  • Mughal paintings were a blend of Indian, Persian, and Islamic styles. They originated from the synthesis of the original Indian style of painting and the Safavid school of Persian painting.
  • Jahangir’s reign saw the full synthesis of Persian and Indian styles of painting. He also inspired his royal studio to take up the single point perspective preferred by European artists.

question: Ashtapradhan was a Council of Minister............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: In the Gupta Administration
option_2: In the Chola Administration
option_3: In the Vijaynagar Administratiom
option_4: In the Maratha Administration
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Ashtapradhan, or Council of Eight, was a council of ministers in the Maratha empire. The Maratha leader Shivaji established the council in 1674.
  • The council is credited with implementing good governance practices and contributing to the success of military campaigns against the Mughal Empire.
  • The eight prominent officials of Marathas were known as Ashta Pradhan.

They were as follows :

1. Peshva (Prime Minister)

2. Amatya or Mazumdar (Finance Minister)

3. Waqia-Navis (like a home minister)

4. Samant or Dabir (dealing with the outsiders)

5. Sachiv (The official correspondence)

6. Pandit Rao (The official religious officer)

7. Sar-i- Nabuat (army affairs)

8. Nyayadhish (judiciary)

The council was responsible for:

  • Negotiating treaties
  • Managing trade relations
  • Representing the kingdom in international forums

question: The head of the military department un der the recorgnised central machinery of administration during Akbar Reign was............. (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Diwan
option_2: Mir Bakshi
option_3: Mir Saman
option_4: Bakshi
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Mir Bakshi was the head of the military department under the recognized central machinery of administration during Akbar’s reign.

Mir Bakshi was also responsible for:

1. Gathering intelligence

2. Recommending military appointments and promotions to the emperor

3. Leading the nobility, information, and intelligence agencies

  • The Mir Bakshi post was established during the Khilji Sultanate of Delhi, but it became more popular and significant during Akbar’s reign.
